proposals.title: Quantifying the Vulnerability of Cold-Cavity Ice Shelves to Future Intrusions of Modified Circumpolar Deep Water
proposals.motivation: The massive, cold-cavity ice shelves of the Ross and Weddell Seas (e.g., Ross, Filchner-Ronne) are currently buttressing vast inland ice, but their stability is contingent on the persistence of cold ocean conditions. Recent modeling (e.g., Hellmer et al., 2012; Naughten et al., 2021) suggests that projected changes in winds and sea ice could alter ocean circulation, potentially allowing warmer Modified Circumpolar Deep Water (mCDW) to access these cavities, which would fundamentally shift their melt regime. Assessing the likelihood and impact of such a regime shift is critical for reducing uncertainty in multi-century sea-level rise projections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a future oceanographic regime shift, driven by changes in atmospheric forcing, will transport mCDW into the Ross and Filchner-Ronne cavities, triggering a non-linear increase in basal melt rates that could destabilize these ice shelves within centuries.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-resolution, coupled ice sheet-ocean model (e.g., MITgcm coupled to ISSM) forced by CMIP6 climate scenarios out to 2300. The model domain will focus on the Ross and Weddell Sea sectors, initialized with the latest Bedmap3 topography and oceanographic observations. We will specifically track the evolution of water mass properties on the continental shelf and their intrusion pathways into the sub-ice cavities. A novel model analysis will quantify the melt sensitivity to incremental changes in offshore mCDW temperature and the resulting changes in grounding line flux using flux-gate methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Antarctic, process-based assessment of the vulnerability of cold-cavity shelves to warm-water intrusion, directly informing the IPCC AR7 and similar assessments. It will deliver quantitative projections of potential future melt rates for these key basins and identify the atmospheric and oceanic precursors that signal an impending regime shift.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have modeled individual basins, this integrated, high-resolution approach across both major cold-cavity systems is novel. It directly tests the stability premise of 'cold-cavity shelves' by simulating the precise oceanographic transitions that could undermine it, moving beyond schematic scenarios to physically realistic forecasts.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Seismic Imaging of Basal Melt Channels and Roughness Beneath the Thwaites Glacier Ice Shelf
proposals.motivation: Basal melt channels and roughness at the ice-ocean interface are critical yet poorly resolved controls on ice-shelf stability, modulating melt rates and structural integrity. Pre-2026 studies, including Rignot's work on Thwaites Glacier, have identified contrasting melt regimes in the grounding zone but lack the fine-scale geophysical constraints needed to link ocean forcing to basal morphology. Directly mapping these features is essential for improving parameterizations in ice-ocean models and reducing uncertainty in projections of Antarctic contribution to sea-level rise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of active and passive seismic methods can resolve the geometry of basal melt channels and quantify basal roughness at sub-meter scales, revealing a direct spatial correlation between channelized features and localized melt anomalies identified from satellite-derived surface elevation changes.
proposals.method: We will deploy dense arrays of geophones and seismometers on the Thwaites Ice Shelf to conduct both active-source seismic reflection surveys (using a hammer or explosive source) and record passive ambient noise. We will employ pre-stack depth migration algorithms to generate high-resolution 3D images of the ice base from reflection data. Passive seismic interferometry will be used to derive shear-wave velocity profiles and constrain ice and water column properties, complementing the active imaging. Basal roughness spectra will be calculated from the interpreted basal reflector using established statistical methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, high-resolution maps of basal channel geometry and roughness beneath a critical Antarctic ice shelf. It will directly validate and refine melt parameterizations used in ocean models like MITgcm and ISOMIP+. The dataset will serve as a crucial benchmark for interpreting satellite altimetry and radar data, advancing the mechanistic understanding of ice-shelf vulnerability.
proposals.novelty: While seismic methods have been used sporadically in glaciology, their systematic application to continuously image basal channels and quantify roughness in 3D beneath a dynamically changing ice shelf is novel. This approach moves beyond the line-of-sight profiling of ice-penetrating radar to provide a volumetric, multi-parameter geophysical characterization of the melt-critical ice-ocean boundary layer.

proposals.title: Direct Quantification of Basal Melt and Cavity Dynamics at Thwaites Glacier Grounding Zone Using In Situ Tracer Release
proposals.motivation: Ocean-driven melting beneath Antarctic ice shelves is a primary control on ice-sheet stability and future sea-level rise, yet direct, in situ measurements of meltwater production and cavity residence times are critically lacking. Existing methods rely on remote sensing or indirect oceanographic inversions, which introduce significant uncertainties in key parameters governing glacier retreat, as highlighted by recent contrasting melt regimes identified at Thwaites Glacier.
proposals.hypothesis: The direct release of conservative tracers into the sub-ice-shelf cavity will yield the first in situ, point-specific meltwater flux and will reveal that meltwater residence times in the grounding zone are shorter than currently modeled, implying a more efficient export of oceanic heat.
proposals.method: We will deploy a borehole-based system to inject a pulse of dissolved, conservative tracers (e.g., sulfur hexafluoride, SF6, and fluorescent dyes) directly into the ocean cavity at the grounding line of Thwaites Glacier, accessed via hot-water drilling. A coordinated array of oceanographic moorings equipped with autonomous samplers and sensors, positioned downstream along the ice shelf base and calibrated using updated Bedmap3 bathymetry, will monitor tracer dispersion and dilution over time. Meltwater production rates will be calculated from the tracer mass balance, while residence times and circulation pathways will be derived from the temporal evolution of the tracer plume using inverse modeling techniques adapted from open-ocean tracer release experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, in situ measurement of basal meltwater production at a critical Antarctic grounding line, delivering a fundamental observational constraint for ice-ocean models. It will uniquely quantify cavity residence times and meltwater export, directly testing mechanisms of dynamic instability initiated by ocean forcing as inferred from historical ice-shelf break-up events.
proposals.novelty: While tracer releases are standard in open oceanography, their application beneath an ice shelf to directly measure melt is unprecedented. This moves beyond indirect remote sensing (e.g., ice surface change) and ship-based ocean profiling to a controlled, point-source experiment within the inaccessible sub-ice cavity.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Modulation of Basal Friction by Subglacial Hydrology and Sediment Deformation in Antarctic Glacier Grounding Zones
proposals.motivation: Projections of Antarctic ice sheet contribution to sea-level rise remain highly uncertain, largely due to poor constraints on basal sliding laws in ice sheet models. While bedrock friction is recognized as a primary control, the specific, spatially variable mechanisms by which subglacial water pressure and deformable sediments modify this friction are poorly quantified, especially in critical and dynamic grounding zones. This gap limits our ability to predict glacier response to oceanic forcing observed in studies of Thwaites and other West Antarctic glaciers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effective basal friction law in Antarctic grounding zones is a composite function, where the bedrock Coulomb friction parameter is dynamically modulated by the local pressure and routing of subglacial meltwater and the thickness and rheology of subglacial sediments.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution observations and inverse modeling. First, we will use updated Bedmap3 bed topography and ice-penetrating radar data to map sediment presence and hydrological potential. Second, we will employ an inverse control method, assimilating long-term surface velocity maps (ITS_LIVE) and ice surface elevation changes (ICESat-2, CryoSat-2) into a higher-order ice flow model to infer spatially distributed basal shear stress. Third, we will statistically analyze the correlation between inferred friction, modeled hydrological pressure, and sediment distribution to derive a parameterized, physics-based modulation of the standard Coulomb friction law.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, observationally constrained parameterization for basal friction that explicitly incorporates hydrological and sedimentary controls. It will directly improve the physical fidelity of ice sheet model projections for key basins like Thwaites and Pine Island. The resulting maps of effective basal state will provide a critical benchmark for interpreting ongoing ocean-driven melt and grounding line retreat.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have separately considered hydrology or sediments, this work uniquely integrates both as explicit modulators of a bedrock-centric friction law, tested directly against continent-wide remote sensing data in active grounding zones, moving beyond idealized theoretical frameworks.

proposals.title: Constraining Ice Sheet Model Initialization and Basal Friction Using High-Resolution Observed Velocity and Bed Topography for Thwaites Glacier
proposals.motivation: Accurate projections of Antarctic ice sheet contribution to sea-level rise are critically dependent on the initial state of numerical ice flow models. Current initialization methods, often reliant on simplified inversions or spin-ups, can produce significant errors in basal traction and ice rheology, leading to large uncertainties in future mass loss forecasts, particularly for vulnerable basins like Thwaites Glacier. Leveraging the new, high-resolution observational datasets now available—specifically the Bedmap3 bed topography and satellite-derived surface velocity maps—provides an unprecedented opportunity to rigorously constrain model initial conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a variational data assimilation approach, which directly inverts for the basal friction coefficient field by minimizing the misfit between modeled and observed surface velocities, will produce a more physically realistic and dynamically consistent initial state for Thwaites Glacier than standard methods, leading to improved near-term forecasts of grounding line retreat.
proposals.method: We will employ the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model (ISSM) framework, utilizing its adjoint-based inversion capabilities. The inversion will be constrained by the ITS_LIVE surface velocity mosaic and the Bedmap3 ice thickness and bed elevation datasets. The cost function will penalize differences between modeled and observed surface velocities, with Tikhonov regularization to ensure a smooth, physically plausible basal friction field. The optimized initial state will then be used to conduct a 50-year forward simulation under projected oceanic forcing scenarios to assess the impact of initialization on grounding line evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a rigorously calibrated, observationally-constrained initial state for the Thwaites Glacier basin, directly addressing a key uncertainty in ice sheet modeling. It will quantify how initialization errors propagate into projections of grounding line retreat and mass loss over decadal timescales, providing a methodological template for other critical Antarctic basins.
proposals.novelty: While inversions for basal conditions are established, this proposal uniquely integrates the latest, highest-resolution continent-wide datasets (Bedmap3, ITS_LIVE) within a state-of-the-art modeling framework (ISSM) specifically for the dynamically complex Thwaites Glacier, focusing on the direct impact of initialization on near-future, ocean-forced retreat forecasts.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Relative Roles of Oceanic Forcing and Ice-Shelf Dynamics in Driving Post-2006 Summer Melt Acceleration in West Antarctica
proposals.motivation: Since 2006, satellite and in-situ observations have documented a significant acceleration in summer melt rates for key Antarctic ice shelves, particularly in the Amundsen Sea sector. This acceleration coincides with a period of pronounced atmospheric warming and changes in Southern Ocean circulation, yet the precise partitioning of climatic versus ice-dynamic drivers remains poorly quantified. Understanding this partitioning is critical for improving projections of ice-shelf stability and future sea-level rise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the post-2006 summer melt acceleration is primarily forced by increased intrusions of modified Circumpolar Deep Water (mCDW) onto the continental shelf, but that this oceanic forcing is non-linearly amplified by dynamic feedbacks within the grounding zone, including enhanced basal crevassing and ice flexure, as revealed by differential satellite interferometry.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-sensor satellite data (ICESat-2, CryoSat-2, Sentinel-1) with updated Bedmap3 topography and oceanographic reanalyses (ECCO, ORAS5) from 2006-2025. A novel, high-resolution melt-rate inversion will be applied to time-series of ice surface elevation and grounding line position. This will be coupled with a statistical attribution analysis using Self-Organizing Maps (SOMs) to correlate melt anomalies with specific modes of atmospheric circulation (e.g., Amundsen Sea Low), ocean thermal content, and locally derived metrics of ice-shelf strain and fracture.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first observationally constrained, mechanistic breakdown of post-2006 summer melt acceleration, separating oceanic heat delivery from ice-dynamic amplification. It will deliver a validated framework for diagnosing melt regimes in key sectors like Thwaites and Pine Island Glaciers, directly informing the next generation of ice-ocean coupled models.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies identify oceanic or atmospheric drivers in isolation, this analysis uniquely fuses high-resolution grounding zone dynamics from differential InSAR with ocean forcing to test for non-linear interactions, moving beyond correlation to diagnose causality in the observed melt acceleration.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Compensatory Role of Refreezing and Snowfall in Modulating Ocean-Forced Mass Loss from the Amundsen Sea Embayment
proposals.motivation: Projections of Antarctic ice sheet mass loss and sea-level rise are dominated by ocean-driven melt at grounding lines. However, surface mass balance (SMB) processes, particularly snowfall and refreezing of meltwater in the firn, act as critical but poorly constrained negative feedbacks. Pre-2026 observations show spatially variable SMB trends and firn air content changes, but their integrated, dynamic impact on the total mass budget of rapidly changing basins like the Amundsen Sea Embayment remains unquantified, leading to uncertainty in decadal-scale projections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increased snowfall and firn refreezing processes have significantly offset a substantial fraction (15-30%) of the ocean-forced dynamic mass loss from key Amundsen Sea glaciers over the past two decades, and that this moderating effect will diminish in future warming scenarios due to firn saturation and rain events.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution regional climate model (RACMO2.3p2) output for snowfall and melt with a firn densification model (IMAU-FDM) to simulate refreezing and firn air content evolution from 2000-2025. These SMB fields will be combined with time series of ice discharge derived from satellite radar interferometry (Sentinel-1) and surface elevation changes from ICESat-2 and CryoSat-2 to solve the mass budget. The analysis will be applied to drainage basins defined using the Bedmap3 bedrock topography, with a focus on the dynamic grounding zones of Thwaites and Pine Island Glaciers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first observationally constrained, basin-scale quantification of how SMB feedbacks modulate net mass loss in Antarctica's most vulnerable sector. It will deliver a novel dataset partitioning mass change into dynamic, snowfall, and refreezing components, directly informing and improving the calibration of ice sheet models used for IPCC projections.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies examine SMB or dynamics in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates firn-process modeling with line-of-sight ice discharge and altimetry data to directly solve the time-evolving mass budget, explicitly isolating the refreezing buffer's role in a region of intense ocean-ice interaction.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Atmospheric and Oceanic Forcings from Partitioned Antarctic Mass Loss Records
proposals.motivation: The Antarctic Ice Sheet is a dominant contributor to sea-level rise, with mass loss driven by complex interactions between atmospheric forcing (surface melt) and oceanic forcing (basal melt). However, direct, long-term observations of these forcings, especially sub-shelf ocean conditions, are spatially and temporally sparse. Inversely estimating these forcings from the well-observed mass loss record would provide critical, observationally-constrained boundary conditions for ice sheet models and improve projections.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatially and temporally resolved record of Antarctic mass loss, partitioned into surface and basal components using methods like surface mass balance and flux gate analyses, contains sufficient information to inversely reconstruct the primary patterns of atmospheric warming and ocean thermal forcing that drove it over the satellite era.
proposals.method: We will use the high-resolution, long-term (1992-2025) partitioned mass loss record (surface mass balance anomaly and ice discharge) from satellite altimetry, gravimetry, and input-output methods as the observational constraint. A Bayesian inverse framework will be employed, using an ensemble of forward simulations from a parameterized ice sheet–shelf model (e.g., based on Ua or ISSM) to define the likelihood. The inversion will optimize for time-varying fields of atmospheric temperature anomaly and ocean thermal forcing at the grounding line by minimizing the misfit between simulated and observed partitioned loss, using efficient sampling techniques (e.g., Markov Chain Monte Carlo) and spatial regularization informed by ocean reanalysis products.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first observationally derived, decadal-scale reconstructions of the dominant atmospheric and oceanic forcings acting on the Antarctic Ice Sheet. It will quantify the relative role of ocean versus atmosphere in driving regional mass loss trends. The resulting forcing estimates will serve as a critical benchmark for validating and calibrating climate models in the polar regions.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate observed loss with climate indices, this proposal formally inverts the ice sheet's mass response to solve for the climatic drivers themselves, treating the ice sheet as a distributed sensor. It integrates Rignot's expertise in partitioning loss and grounding line processes into a unified inverse framework, moving beyond forward attribution.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Controls on Antarctic Ice Flow: A Rheology-First Inversion of Ice Shelf and Grounding Zone Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Contemporary ice flow models for Antarctica heavily parameterize basal drag as the primary control on ice motion, often treating ice rheology as a secondary, spatially homogeneous factor. This assumption is increasingly challenged by observations of complex flow patterns in ice shelves and grounding zones, where lateral drag and longitudinal stresses are significant. A systematic, continent-scale investigation into the relative roles of bed topography (via basal drag) versus ice stiffness (via rheology) is critical for improving projections of sea-level rise, especially for rapidly changing basins like Thwaites and Pine Island.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for major Antarctic outlet glaciers and their adjoining ice shelves, spatial variations in ice rheology (stiffness), governed by temperature, fabric, and damage, exert a control on ice motion comparable to or greater than that of basal drag in key dynamic regions, particularly within and downstream of the grounding zone.
proposals.method: We will employ an inverse modeling framework using the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model (ISSM), constrained by the latest surface velocity mosaics (NASA ITS_LIVE) and the new high-resolution Bedmap3 topography. The inversion will be performed in two stages: first, a conventional inversion for basal friction; second, a novel simultaneous inversion for both basal friction and a spatially variable ice rheology parameter (flow rate factor, A). We will utilize a control method with Tikhonov regularization, specifically testing the L-curve criterion to balance data misfit against parameter smoothness. The analysis will focus on contrasting regions (e.g., Thwaites vs. Ross ice streams) to identify where rheological control dominates.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Antarctic map quantifying the relative contribution of rheology versus basal drag to ice motion. It will directly improve the physical fidelity of ice sheet models by providing a new, observationally constrained parameterization for ice stiffness. The results will identify which glaciers are most sensitive to changes in ice temperature and fabric, informing priorities for future thermomechanical modeling and observational campaigns.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have inverted for rheology in limited domains, a systematic, continent-scale dual inversion for both friction and rheology—explicitly testing their relative dominance—is novel. This approach relaxes the foundational model assumption of rheological homogeneity, directly addressing a key uncertainty in ice sheet projections highlighted by the IPCC AR6.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Mapping of Antarctic Subglacial Hydrology Pathways Using Bedmap3 Topography and Ice-Shelf Melt Forcing
proposals.motivation: The newly released Bedmap3 dataset provides unprecedented detail of Antarctic subglacial topography, revealing complex trough and ridge structures that fundamentally control subglacial water flow. This water flow is a critical but poorly constrained component of ice-sheet dynamics, influencing basal lubrication, sediment transport, and the thermal and mechanical coupling at the ice-bed interface, particularly in grounding zones vulnerable to ocean-driven melt.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high-resolution bed roughness and macro-scale structures in Bedmap3 will reveal persistent, organized drainage pathways that channel meltwater, and that the spatial pattern of this drainage is a primary control on the heterogeneity of basal shear stress and grounding-line melt rates observed at major outlet glaciers like Thwaites.
proposals.method: We will employ a physically based subglacial hydrological model (e.g., GlaDS or a similar sheet-conduit model) forced with meltwater input derived from satellite-derived surface melt and modeled basal melt rates near grounding lines. The model will be initialized on the Bedmap3 grid, explicitly incorporating its updated bed elevation, ice thickness, and surface elevation. Key algorithms will include the computation of hydraulic potential from the Bedmap3 geometry and the iterative solution of water flow through linked distributed and channelized systems. The model output will be validated against observed patterns of basal sliding and grounding-line retreat from InSAR and ICESat-2 data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first continent-wide, high-resolution map of potential subglacial drainage pathways consistent with the Bedmap3 topography. It will directly quantify how subglacial water modulates ice flow into vulnerable ice shelves and provide a mechanistic framework to interpret the contrasting melt regimes observed in grounding zones, thereby improving projections of future ice-sheet contribution to sea-level rise.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have modeled subglacial hydrology, none have yet leveraged the full resolution and accuracy of the Bedmap3 topography to map drainage systems at this scale, nor explicitly linked the revealed bed structures to the spatially variable ocean melt forcing that is a focus of Rignot's recent work on grounding-zone dynamics.

proposals.title: Decadal-Scale Evolution of Subglacial Topography in Antarctic Sedimentary Basins: Inferences from Inverse Methods and Multi-Epoch Observations
proposals.motivation: Current ice sheet models and mass balance assessments, including those based on Bedmap3, assume a static subglacial bed. This assumption is likely invalid in sedimentary basins where subglacial erosion, deposition, and deformation can be significant. Neglecting this dynamic topography introduces errors in projections of grounding line migration and ice sheet stability, particularly for slow-moving ice streams where subtle changes can precondition future rapid retreat.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subglacial bed elevation in Antarctic sedimentary basins has changed measurably over recent decades due to dynamic sedimentary processes, and that these changes can be detected by combining multi-epoch observations with inverse methods.
proposals.method: We will apply a Bayesian inverse model to repeated surface elevation data (from ICESat, ICESat-2, and Operation IceBridge) and ice velocity data (from InSAR) to solve for time-dependent changes in bed elevation and slipperiness. The method builds on the control inverse approach used in Rignot et al. (2014) but extends it to a multi-temporal inversion, using Bedmap3 as a prior. We will focus on the slow-moving interior catchments feeding the Amundsen Sea Embayment and the Siple Coast, where sedimentary basins are prevalent.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first continent-wide assessment of potential decadal-scale subglacial topographic change in Antarctica. It will quantify a previously unmodeled source of uncertainty in ice sheet models and provide new insights into the geomorphic processes operating beneath the ice sheet, with direct implications for improving projections of sea-level rise.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have inferred bed properties or single-epoch topography, the explicit inversion for *temporal change* in bed elevation over decadal scales is novel. This challenges the foundational static-bed assumption in glaciology by directly testing it with a rigorous, physics-based methodology.

proposals.title: Inverting for Antarctic Basal Topography and Slipperiness Using High-Resolution Surface Observations and an Adjoint Ice Flow Model
proposals.motivation: Accurate knowledge of bed topography and basal slipperiness is critical for modeling ice sheet stability and projecting sea-level rise, yet direct observations beneath ice sheets are sparse. The recent availability of continent-wide, high-resolution surface velocity (ITS_LIVE) and elevation (e.g., Bedmap3, ICESat-2) datasets for Antarctica presents a major opportunity to constrain these basal properties indirectly. This is particularly urgent for rapidly changing regions like the Amundsen Sea Embayment, where bed geometry controls marine ice sheet instability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a full-Stokes, depth-integrated ice flow model, coupled with an adjoint-based inverse method, can simultaneously and robustly infer high-resolution basal topography and slipperiness fields from surface velocity and elevation data, reducing uncertainties in key grounding zone regions.
proposals.method: We will employ the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model (ISSM) and its adjoint capabilities to solve the inverse problem. The method minimizes a cost function quantifying misfit between modeled and observed surface velocities (from ITS_LIVE) and surface elevations (aligned with Bedmap3), regularized by prior constraints from sparse bed echoes. The adjoint method efficiently computes the gradient of this cost function with respect to the basal boundary conditions (topography and friction coefficient), enabling a large-scale optimization. The inversion will be performed at 500m resolution for focused domains, leveraging long-term surface motion records to constrain transient effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce novel, observationally constrained maps of bed topography and slipperiness in critical Antarctic sectors, directly updating and improving upon datasets like Bedmap3. It will provide a validated methodological framework for assimilating surface remote-sensing data into ice flow models, leading to more reliable projections of grounding line retreat and ice discharge.
proposals.novelty: While inversions for basal slipperiness are common, the simultaneous, adjoint-based inversion for both topography *and* slipperiness at high resolution across dynamic grounding zones is a significant advance beyond current piecemeal or sequential approaches, directly addressing the coupled nature of the basal boundary problem.

proposals.title: Testing the Irreversibility of Antarctic Glacier Acceleration: A Multi-Basin Analysis of Stabilization Potential
proposals.motivation: Recent observations, particularly in the Amundsen Sea Embayment, show sustained acceleration and grounding line retreat of major Antarctic glaciers, raising concerns of a committed, irreversible contribution to sea-level rise. However, the potential for dynamic stabilization or deceleration under evolving ocean forcing and bed geometry remains a critical uncertainty for century-scale projections. This work directly addresses whether observed acceleration trends are monotonic or if internal glacier dynamics and feedbacks could permit a return to a slower state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for key Antarctic basins, once a glacier's grounding line retreats past a subglacial bed down-sloping (retrograde) threshold, the acceleration trend becomes effectively irreversible under plausible 21st-century climate scenarios, with no dynamic stabilization possible.
proposals.method: We will employ an observational-modeling framework. First, we will generate a multi-decadal record (1990s-2025) of ice velocity and grounding line position for all major Antarctic basins using a consistent processing chain of satellite radar data (ERS, ENVISAT, Sentinel-1). Second, we will use the high-resolution Bedmap3 dataset to map bed slopes and identify potential stabilizing (prograde) features. Third, we will use a flowline model, calibrated with observed acceleration and retreat, to conduct perturbation experiments testing if simulated reductions in ocean melt can halt retreat or reverse acceleration for glaciers with varying bed geometries.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a pan-Antarctic assessment of which glacier basins are most susceptible to irreversible acceleration, moving beyond case studies like Thwaites. It will deliver a empirically constrained, mechanistic classification of glacier stability based on observed retreat history and bed topography, directly informing the physical basis for 'tipping point' projections in ice sheet models.
proposals.novelty: While past work identifies retrograde beds as unstable, this study uniquely tests the reversibility of acceleration using observed, long-term velocity trends as a constraint, and systematically searches for potential natural brakes (e.g., bedrock highs) that could allow for stabilization, a non-obvious possibility in rapidly changing systems.

proposals.title: Sub-Satellite-Scale Melt Heterogeneity as a Modulator of Large-Scale Ice Shelf and Glacier Acceleration in Antarctica
proposals.motivation: Satellite observations have quantified the large-scale acceleration and thinning of Antarctic outlet glaciers, primarily linking them to ocean-driven melt at the grounding line. However, the integrated satellite signal may average over critical sub-kilometer-scale processes in the grounding zone, such as localized basal channel melt, ice shelf flexure, and small-scale ocean circulation. Understanding whether and how these unresolved processes modulate the large-scale dynamic response is critical for improving the predictive skill of ice sheet models, which currently parameterize these effects coarsely.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatial and temporal heterogeneity in sub-satellite-scale basal melt within the grounding zone and ice shelf cavity is a primary modulator of the large-scale acceleration signal observed by satellites, with heterogeneity amplifying or dampening the glacier's response to ocean forcing depending on its spatial pattern relative to bedrock pinning points.
proposals.method: We will leverage high-resolution (2-16 m) stereo and InSAR-derived surface elevation and velocity maps from NASA's ITS_LIVE, ICESat-2, and TanDEM-X missions, focusing on the Amundsen Sea Embayment (Thwaites, Pine Island). These will be combined with the new Bedmap3 bathymetry and ice geometry to identify sub-scale features (melt channels, rifts, pinning points). A multi-temporal correlation analysis will be performed between maps of localized melt variability (inferred from surface elevation change) and subsequent changes in ice velocity and strain rates at the satellite-observable scale, using a deep learning architecture (a Convolutional Neural Network) trained to detect causal links between fine-scale melt patterns and larger-scale dynamic response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first observational framework to quantitatively link sub-satellite-scale melt processes to continental-scale glacier dynamics. It will deliver a novel, observationally constrained parameterization for how melt heterogeneity influences grounding line stability and ice shelf buttressing, directly informing next-generation ice sheet models like ISSM and BISICLES.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have identified small-scale melt features, this proposal uniquely seeks to statistically quantify their integrated impact on the large-scale acceleration signal that drives sea-level rise projections, moving from descriptive mapping to mechanistic attribution.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Dominance of Dynamic Thinning in Antarctic Mass Loss: A Multi-Mission, Multi-Decadal Analysis
proposals.motivation: Antarctic ice mass loss is a primary contributor to global sea-level rise, but the relative contributions of surface mass balance (SMB) changes versus ice-dynamic discharge remain poorly quantified at the continental scale. This separation is critical for improving model projections, as dynamic losses are driven by oceanic forcing at grounding lines and represent a potentially irreversible, accelerating component of mass loss. A comprehensive, observation-based partitioning is urgently needed to attribute past changes and constrain future scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic thinning, driven by ocean-induced melt at grounding lines and subsequent ice shelf disintegration, accounts for more than 70% of Antarctica's cumulative mass deficit since the early 1990s, with its relative dominance increasing in recent decades.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-mission satellite data from 1992-2025. Ice sheet mass change will be derived from GRACE/GRACE-FO and ICESat/ICESat-2 altimetry, reconciled via the Input-Output Method. Surface mass balance will be obtained from high-resolution regional climate model outputs (RACMO2.3p2, MAR). Dynamic mass loss (discharge minus SMB) will be calculated as the residual. We will apply this framework at the basin scale, using updated Bedmap3 topography to accurately define drainage basins and grounding line positions from InSAR. Time series analysis will isolate trends and accelerations in each component.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first continent-wide, multi-decadal quantification of the partitioned mass loss, directly testing the 'dynamic thinning' paradigm. It will produce a publicly available dataset of SMB and dynamic mass change by basin, serving as a benchmark for ice sheet models. The results will formally attribute observed accelerations to specific physical processes, informing IPCC assessments and mitigation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies note dynamic thinning in specific sectors, a systematic, observation-based continental partitioning using the latest topography (Bedmap3) and full ICESat-2 record has not been completed. The novel integration of altimetric thickness change with GRACE and modeled SMB at basin scale provides a robust closure of the mass budget.

proposals.title: Diagnosing the Trigger of Antarctic Glacier Acceleration: A Network Cascade Analysis of Spatiotemporal Velocity Patterns
proposals.motivation: The widespread acceleration of Antarctic glaciers, particularly in the Amundsen Sea Embayment, is a primary contributor to sea-level rise. A critical unresolved question is whether this acceleration is driven by localized, chain-reaction destabilizations initiated at a single glacier (e.g., Pine Island) or by synchronous, widespread oceanic or atmospheric forcing across the region. Distinguishing between these mechanisms is essential for improving projections of ice-sheet stability and sea-level rise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed acceleration patterns in West Antarctica are more consistent with a network cascade process initiated at a key node (e.g., Pine Island Glacier), propagating via mechanical and oceanic connections, rather than with a uniform, region-wide environmental forcing.
proposals.method: We will construct a glacier terminus network for West Antarctica, where nodes are glacier grounding lines and edges represent potential pathways for stress or ocean-driven perturbation propagation, informed by Bedmap3 subglacial topography and bathymetry. Using long-term, high-resolution satellite-derived surface velocity time series (e.g., ITS_LIVE), we will adapt statistical cascade detection methods from epidemiology, such as the Independent Cascade Model and source inference algorithms, to analyze the spatiotemporal sequence of speed-up events. This will involve fitting models to determine if the observed velocity changes are best explained by a single-origin cascade or a multi-origin synchronous pattern.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework to diagnose the dominant trigger mechanism for regional glacier acceleration, directly informing debates on ice-sheet vulnerability. It will produce a new diagnostic metric for evaluating ice-sheet models and identify which glaciers are potential 'patient zeros' for regional instability, refining risk assessments for sea-level rise.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of formal network cascade and source detection frameworks from epidemiology to glaciology, a methodological crossover not yet applied to diagnose the physical triggers of ice-sheet change. This moves beyond correlation analyses to a physics-informed, statistical inference of causation in spatiotemporal acceleration patterns.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Mapping of Basal Slip Heterogeneity on Thwaites Glacier Using Dense Seismic Arrays and Acoustic Emission Source Location Techniques
proposals.motivation: The dynamic instability of Antarctic glaciers like Thwaites is primarily driven by ocean-induced melt at the grounding line and changes in basal resistance. However, direct observation of the spatiotemporal patterns of basal slip—a key control on ice flow—remains a major observational gap. Pre-2026, while seismic methods have detected glacial stick-slip and tremor, they lack the resolution to map the detailed 3D structure and mechanics of the basal sliding regime.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that discrete basal slip events generate high-frequency seismic signals whose precise 3D source locations and mechanisms, resolved using adapted acoustic emission techniques, will reveal a heterogeneous map of basal friction and stress transfer that correlates with ice flow variability and subglacial hydrology.
proposals.method: We will deploy a dense, short-period seismic array (~50 stations, 500m spacing) on the floating and grounded ice of Thwaites Glacier for one melt season. We will detect high-frequency icequakes and tremor using automated algorithms. For event location, we will apply a double-difference seismic tomography algorithm to refine relative hypocenters, followed by time-reversal source imaging to characterize extended sources. Moment tensor inversion will be performed on located events to discriminate between shear (stick-slip) and tensile (hydraulic) mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution 3D map of basal slip event distribution and source mechanics for a major Antarctic glacier. It will directly quantify the spatial heterogeneity of basal friction and its temporal evolution in response to meltwater forcing, providing a novel, process-based constraint for ice sheet models that currently parameterize basal sliding poorly.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic adaptation of high-precision acoustic emission source location and characterization methods from structural engineering to glaciology, moving beyond simple event detection to deliver a quantitative, mechanistic map of basal sliding at sub-kilometer scale, which has not been achieved before.

proposals.title: Causal Attribution of Dynamic Instability in Antarctic Ice Shelves: Integrating Breakpoint Detection with Structural Causal Models
proposals.motivation: Recent studies, including Rignot's work on Thwaites Glacier and Wordie Ice Shelf, reveal complex, non-linear ice velocity responses to oceanic and atmospheric forcing. Standard statistical correlations cannot distinguish coincident timing from causal mechanisms, hindering process-based understanding and predictive capability for sea-level rise. A robust framework is needed to formally test causal hypotheses linking observed velocity breakpoints to specific physical drivers.
proposals.hypothesis: Statistically significant breakpoints in Antarctic ice velocity time series can be causally attributed to specific, identifiable physical forcings (e.g., ocean thermal forcing at the grounding line, ice shelf thinning) when analyzed within a physics-constrained structural causal model.
proposals.method: We will apply econometric breakpoint detection algorithms (e.g., Bai-Perron test) to multi-decadal ice velocity maps (e.g., MEaSUREs) to identify potential change points. For each candidate breakpoint, we will construct a Structural Causal Model (SCM) representing hypothesized causal pathways from pre-2026 datasets: ocean temperature/salinity (ECCO, EN4), ice shelf bathymetry/thinning (Bedmap3, ICESat-2/Operation IceBridge), and atmospheric reanalysis (ERA5). Using causal inference methods (e.g., do-calculus, backdoor adjustment), we will test the necessity and sufficiency of each driver, filtering the breakpoint list to those with a physically explicable causal attribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, causally-interpretable catalog of ice dynamic change events across Antarctica, directly linking them to environmental drivers. It will advance the field from descriptive correlation to hypothesis-driven causal analysis, providing a rigorous framework for validating ice sheet model processes and improving projections of ice sheet contribution to sea level.
proposals.novelty: The integration of econometric breakpoint analysis with formal causal inference methods, explicitly constrained by glaciological principles and high-resolution physical datasets, represents a significant methodological advance beyond conventional time-series correlation studies in glaciology.

proposals.title: Detecting Regime Shifts in Antarctic Ice Flow: A Change-Point Analysis Framework Adapted from Hydrology
proposals.motivation: Ice velocity time-series from satellites are crucial for diagnosing glacier stability, but current analyses often treat trends as monolithic, obscuring the timing and magnitude of discrete dynamical shifts. These shifts—triggered by ice shelf disintegration, meltwater pulses, or grounding line retreat—are key to projecting sea-level rise. A systematic method to partition velocity records into distinct dynamical regimes is needed to link external forcings to ice sheet response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that change-point detection methods, proven in hydrology to separate climatic and anthropogenic signals, can robustly identify significant breakpoints in ice velocity records, and that the segmented trends between these breakpoints correspond to distinct physical forcing regimes (e.g., stable flow, ocean-driven acceleration, post-calving deceleration).
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-method framework to multi-decadal ice velocity time-series (e.g., from MEaSUREs ITS_LIVE) for key Antarctic glaciers. This includes non-parametric tests (Pettitt test), segmentation algorithms (e.g., Pruned Exact Linear Time - PELT), and Bayesian change point models. The methods will be adapted to handle glaciological noise characteristics. Identified breakpoints will be validated against known events (calving, shelf collapse) from remote sensing, and segmented linear trends will be analyzed in the context of ocean thermal and atmospheric forcing data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, standardized methodology for diagnosing dynamical transitions in ice flow, moving beyond simple linear trend analysis. It will produce a quantified catalog of breakpoints and regime-specific trends for major Antarctic outlets, directly informing ice sheet models about the timing and magnitude of glacier sensitivity to external drivers.
proposals.novelty: While change-point analysis is common in hydrology and econometrics, its systematic application to ice velocity time-series is novel. The adaptation involves tailoring these methods to the specific noise structure and physical drivers of glacier flow, creating a direct analogy between 'impacted' periods in river discharge and 'forced' periods in ice dynamics.

proposals.title: Quantifying Regime Shifts in Antarctic Ice Flow: An Econometric Time-Series Analysis of Glacier Velocity and Ocean Forcing
proposals.motivation: Recent studies, including those on Thwaites Glacier, reveal complex, non-linear ice flow responses to ocean-driven melt, challenging linear projections of sea-level rise. The precise timing and magnitude of dynamic transitions—such as grounding line retreat, ice shelf break-up, and subsequent acceleration—remain poorly quantified. A robust, statistical framework is needed to objectively identify these critical breakpoints and trends in observational records to improve ice sheet model initialization and validation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that econometric breakpoint detection algorithms will reveal statistically significant, co-temporal shifts in ice velocity time series across multiple glaciers following major oceanic or mechanical perturbations (e.g., shelf loss). Further, we hypothesize that the post-breakpoint trend in acceleration will correlate with regional ocean thermal forcing and bed topography.
proposals.method: We will apply the Bai-Perron multiple breakpoint test and Zivot-Andrews unit root test to multi-decadal, continent-wide ice velocity mosaics (e.g., ITS_LIVE) to identify structural changes in flow regimes. For key basins (Amundsen Sea, Antarctic Peninsula), we will correlate breakpoints with time series of ocean temperature (from ECCO2 or instrumental data) and grounding line position (from differential satellite radar interferometry). The analysis will leverage long-term surface elevation and bed topography data from Bedmap3 to contextualize the geophysical drivers of identified regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, objective catalog of dynamic instability events in Antarctica, quantifying their timing and associated changes in flow trend. It will directly inform ice sheet models by identifying observed, non-linear thresholds for parameterization. The methodology will establish a new framework for diagnosing the onset of glacier instability from remote sensing data.
proposals.novelty: While breakpoint analysis is standard in econometrics, its application to geophysical time series of ice flow is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive analysis to provide statistical rigor in pinpointing the timing of regime shifts and testing their correlation with external forcings, a method not yet adopted in glaciology.

proposals.title: A Pan-Arctic Discharge Synthesis Engine (PADSE): A Data-Fusion Framework for Coherent, Uncertainty-Aware Ice Discharge Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Quantifying pan-Arctic ice discharge is critical for constraining sea-level rise projections and understanding cryosphere-ocean interactions. Current assessments are fragmented, relying on disparate methods and data sources for individual glaciers, leading to inconsistencies and large uncertainties when scaling to the entire Arctic. A unified, scalable framework is needed to synthesize heterogeneous observations into a coherent, continuous, and uncertainty-quantified estimate of total Arctic ice mass flux.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian data fusion framework, integrating satellite-derived surface velocity and elevation, modeled ice thickness, and sparse in-situ measurements, can produce a more accurate, consistent, and uncertainty-aware pan-Arctic ice discharge time series than the simple summation of individually derived glacier estimates.
proposals.method: We will develop the Pan-Arctic Discharge Synthesis Engine (PADSE), adapting principles from ecosystem sensor network synthesis. The core is a hierarchical Bayesian model that fuses data streams: satellite ice velocity (e.g., Sentinel-1), surface elevation (ICESat-2, CryoSat-2), ice thickness from BedMachine and models, and terminus positions. The framework will use Gaussian Process regression to interpolate data gaps and Kalman filtering for temporal synthesis, explicitly propagating errors from input data through to final discharge estimates for each glacier and the integrated total.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first internally consistent, uncertainty-bounded time series of pan-Arctic ice discharge, directly addressing methodological inconsistencies in upscaling. It will provide a foundational dataset for model validation and a scalable operational framework for continuous monitoring, significantly improving the reliability of Arctic contributions to sea-level budgets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a formal data-assimilation and sensor-network synthesis framework—common in other geophysical fields—to the pan-cryospheric scale discharge problem, moving beyond simple averaging of disparate products to a statistically rigorous, physics-informed integration of all available data.

proposals.title: Bayesian Ensemble Inversion of Basal and Oceanic Forcings for Thwaites Glacier Using Physics-Constrained Priors and Time-Dependent Surface Velocities
proposals.motivation: The rapid, accelerating retreat of Thwaites Glacier is a primary contributor to global sea-level rise, yet its dynamics remain poorly constrained due to the ill-posed nature of inferring basal sliding and sub-shelf melt from surface observations. Traditional inverse methods produce non-unique solutions with unquantified uncertainties, limiting predictive confidence for ice sheet models. This project directly addresses this critical gap by developing a robust probabilistic framework to map the forcings driving Thwaites' instability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian ensemble approach, constrained by physical laws and multi-source prior information (e.g., Bedmap3 bathymetry, inferred melt regimes), can invert time-series of surface velocity to produce a posterior distribution of basal and oceanic forcings that is both physically consistent and quantifiably uncertain.
proposals.method: We will employ the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model (ISSM) to generate a prior ensemble of plausible basal friction and sub-shelf melt rate fields, constrained by physical parameterizations and auxiliary data from recent studies of the Thwaites grounding zone. Using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm, we will assimilate multi-year time-series of surface velocity (derived from satellite interferometry and feature tracking) to update the ensemble weights, producing a posterior probability distribution. The inversion will leverage the adjoint capabilities of ISSM for efficient gradient computations within the Bayesian framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first probabilistically rigorous, spatially complete maps of the basal and oceanic forcings beneath Thwaites Glacier, complete with formal uncertainty estimates. It will directly inform projections of future retreat by providing better-constrained initial and boundary conditions for prognostic ice sheet models, moving beyond best-estimate inversions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a high-fidelity ice dynamics model within a formal Bayesian ensemble inversion scheme specifically designed for the Antarctic grounding zone, moving from deterministic to probabilistic forcing estimates while explicitly incorporating time-dependent observational constraints.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Glacial Hydrological Pulse: A High-Resolution, Multi-Sensor Analysis of Sub-Annual Ice Flow Dynamics in West Antarctica
proposals.motivation: The dynamic response of Antarctic glaciers to meltwater input is a critical but poorly resolved component of sea-level rise projections. Pre-2026 studies using annual or seasonal velocity averages obscure the mechanistic links between discrete hydrological pulses and ice flow acceleration, particularly in critical regions like the Amundsen Sea Embayment. Understanding the precise temporal phasing and causality within a melt season is essential for improving the physical realism of ice sheet models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the seasonal velocity cycle of a major West Antarctic glacier can be decomposed into distinct, repeatable phases analogous to a cardiac cycle, where specific timings and magnitudes of surface meltwater pulses trigger predictable pressurization and evolution of the subglacial drainage system, leading to quantifiable ice flow responses.
proposals.method: We will deploy a dense, synchronized sensor array on a key glacier (e.g., Thwaites or Pine Island) for a full annual cycle, integrating high-rate GPS for velocity, seismometers for basal activity, borehole pressure sensors, and proximal discharge measurements. Data streams will be co-registered in time and analyzed using signal processing techniques adapted from cardiology, specifically phase-domain analysis and cross-correlation, to identify characteristic waveforms and establish lag times between melt input, basal pressure, and velocity peaks. This will be coupled with existing remote sensing melt and ice surface datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first mechanistic, phase-resolved model of sub-annual ice flow response to hydrology, directly linking process to observation. It will deliver critical observational constraints on the timing and efficiency of subglacial drainage activation and its impact on ice dynamics, providing a new framework for evaluating and improving subglacial hydrology parameterizations in ice sheet models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual and analytical adaptation of cardiac cycle analysis to glaciology, shifting the focus from seasonal averages to the precise event-scale chronology of the glacial hydrological system. This approach moves beyond correlative studies to establish temporal causality using a synchronized, multi-physics sensor suite at unprecedented sub-daily resolution for Antarctic ice streams.

proposals.title: Seasonal Modulation of Ice Flow in the Amundsen Sea Embayment: Linking Sub-Annual Velocity Cycles to Meltwater Hydrology and Ocean Forcing
proposals.motivation: Recent work, including Rignot et al.'s studies on Thwaites Glacier, has established that ocean-driven melt at grounding lines is a primary driver of long-term Antarctic ice sheet retreat. However, the sub-annual, seasonal-scale processes that modulate ice flow and potentially precondition glaciers for instability are poorly quantified. Understanding these high-frequency cycles is critical for improving projections of sea-level rise, as they may influence ice shelf buttressing and grounding line stability on actionable timescales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that seasonal variations in ice flow velocity for major Amundsen Sea glaciers (Thwaites, Pine Island) are directly forced by a combination of (1) surface meltwater-induced basal lubrication in the upper catchment and (2) tidally-modulated ocean melt and subglacial discharge pulses near the grounding line, with their relative importance varying spatially across the glacier system.
proposals.method: We will generate high-resolution, sub-annual (monthly to bi-monthly) ice velocity mosaics for 2014-2025 using dense time-series processing of Sentinel-1 A/B SAR data via the MIT Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (ISCE2) stack and a feature-tracking algorithm (autoRIFT). These velocity maps will be coregistered to Bedmap3 topography and analyzed alongside concurrent records of: surface melt from regional climate models (RACMO2.3p2), ocean thermal forcing from in-situ and satellite-derived data, and tidal phase. A multivariate statistical model will isolate the hydrological and oceanic drivers of the observed velocity cycles.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, observationally-based quantification of seasonal ice flow dynamics for the vulnerable Amundsen Sea sector. It will directly constrain the sensitivity of glacier flow to short-term environmental forcing, providing essential validation for ice sheet models that are beginning to incorporate sub-annual hydrology. The findings will clarify whether seasonal signals amplify or dampen long-term retreat trends.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have identified seasonal signals, this analysis uniquely integrates the full glacier system from the interior to the grounding zone, leveraging updated Bedmap3 geometry to precisely link velocity variations to specific hydrological and oceanic processes in space and time, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic insight.

proposals.title: Dynamic Bed Inversion: Constraining Rate-and-State Friction at the Base of Thwaites Glacier Using High-Frequency Ice Velocity and Tidal Forcing
proposals.motivation: Traditional inversions for basal slipperiness assume a static, unique relationship between driving stress and velocity, which fails to capture the transient, rate-dependent frictional behavior of glacier beds. This is a critical limitation for predicting the dynamic instability of key Antarctic outlets like Thwaites Glacier, where ocean-driven melt at the grounding line modulates basal stress. A new paradigm is needed to directly probe the constitutive properties of the bed.
proposals.hypothesis: High-frequency time-series of ice surface velocity, forced by ocean tides, contain a spectral and phase signature that can be inverted to constrain rate-and-state friction parameters at the glacier bed, moving beyond a static slipperiness to a dynamic friction law.
proposals.method: We will utilize existing multi-year, high-rate GPS records and dense Sentinel-1 SAR time series from the Thwaites Glacier grounding zone. We will develop an inverse model that couples a linearized or full-Stokes ice flow model with a rate-and-state friction law. The inversion will fit not just mean velocities but the amplitude and phase lag of the observed tidal harmonic constituents in the velocity signal, using an adjoint-based optimization framework to solve for the friction parameters (reference friction, state evolution distance) and their spatial distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first map of dynamic friction parameters for a major Antarctic glacier, fundamentally advancing the physical representation of the bed in ice sheet models. It will provide a direct observational constraint on how basal friction evolves in response to tidal forcing and meltwater pressure, improving projections of grounding line retreat and sea-level rise.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in glaciology, adapting the core concept of seismic waveform inversion for fault friction to the ice stream environment. It treats velocity time-series as a dynamic signal for bed characterization, challenging the foundational assumption of a static stress-velocity relationship in all existing bed inversion schemes.

proposals.title: High-Resolution 3D Inversion of the Thwaites Glacier Grounding Zone: Imaging Structural Complexity and Its Control on Melt Regimes
proposals.motivation: The grounding line is the critical boundary controlling ice sheet stability, yet its physical structure is poorly resolved. Pre-2026 observations, including Rignot's work on Thwaites, reveal contrasting melt regimes that suggest complex sub-ice topography and hydrology, not captured by simple bed-topography merges. This structural complexity likely governs ocean heat access and ice dynamics, representing a key uncertainty in sea-level rise projections.
proposals.hypothesis: The grounding zone is not a simple, planar interface but a structurally complex 3D volume, analogous to a seismic fault zone, where features like subglacial channels, cavities, and pinning points dictate spatially heterogeneous basal melt and mechanical coupling.
proposals.method: We will deploy a dense, multi-instrument network (phase-sensitive radar, passive seismometers, GPS, ApRES) across a 10x10 km grounding zone of Thwaites Glacier. Data will be fused into a joint geophysical inversion framework, adapting seismic full-waveform inversion and electrical resistivity tomography algorithms. This will invert for a high-resolution 3D model of ice-bed-ocean interface geometry, material properties (till, water, rock), and basal traction.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first explicit 3D structural map of a critical Antarctic grounding zone, directly linking geophysical features to observed melt heterogeneity. It will provide a new mechanistic framework for grounding line processes, improving parameterizations of melt and friction in ice sheet models for more accurate projections of glacier retreat and sea-level rise.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally shifts the conceptual model of the grounding line from a 2D boundary to a 3D structural zone, applying advanced seismic imaging paradigms to glaciology. It moves beyond inferring processes from sparse lines to direct volumetric imaging of the interface controlling ice-ocean interaction.

proposals.title: Network Flow Analysis of Antarctic Seabed Pathways to Quantify Oceanic Heat Delivery and Glacier Vulnerability
proposals.motivation: The rapid retreat of key Antarctic glaciers like Thwaites is primarily driven by oceanic melt, yet the pathways and controls on the delivery of warm Circumpolar Deep Water (CDW) to grounding zones remain poorly quantified. New high-resolution bathymetric compilations (e.g., Bedmap3, IBCSO V2) now reveal the complex seabed topography that steers these flows, but a systematic framework to identify critical bottlenecks and predict vulnerability is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the delivery of oceanic heat to Antarctic grounding zones can be modeled as a network flow problem, where specific seabed features (e.g., sills, canyons) act as critical links whose functional capacity controls melt rates and determines the vulnerability of downstream glacier basins to changes in oceanic forcing.
proposals.method: We will construct a directed network from high-resolution bathymetry, where nodes represent defined seabed areas and edges represent viable pathways for CDW, weighted by an effective hydraulic conductance. Using adapted network flow algorithms (e.g., Edmonds-Karp for max-flow/min-cut analysis), we will model oceanic heat as 'traffic' to identify bottlenecks controlling flow to specific glacier cavities. We will then perform link failure and capacity change simulations, informed by projected ocean warming and sediment-driven bathymetric evolution, to assess how network alterations redistribute heat delivery.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first physics-informed vulnerability index for Antarctic glaciers based on the criticality of their upstream oceanic heat delivery pathways. It will deliver a scalable framework to prioritize observational and modeling efforts on the seabed features and glaciers most susceptible to near-term changes in ocean circulation.
proposals.novelty: While oceanographic tracing studies exist, applying formal network flow theory and critical path analysis from engineering to the problem of oceanic heat transport to glaciers is a novel, systems-level approach. It moves beyond correlative studies to a dynamic, mechanistic assessment of vulnerability based on network topology and flow capacity.

proposals.title: Synergistic Validation of Antarctic Bed Topography and Subglacial Hydrology Using Flow Routing and Targeted Geophysical Surveys
proposals.motivation: Accurate bed topography beneath the Antarctic Ice Sheet, as provided by BedMachine v3, is critical for modeling ice flow and projecting sea-level rise. However, its resolution and accuracy in key regions like grounding zones remain uncertain, directly impacting predictions of glacier stability. Simultaneously, the structure and evolution of subglacial hydrological systems, which modulate basal lubrication and ocean-driven melt, are poorly constrained by direct observations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating landscape evolution-based subglacial flow routing models with targeted geophysical validation creates a synergistic cycle: predicted drainage pathways efficiently identify locations where bed topography errors are most likely, and new observations simultaneously validate the DEM and calibrate the hydrological model, improving the physical representation of both systems.
proposals.method: We will apply the Python-based terrain analysis package `pysheds` to perform flow accumulation and channel extraction on the BedMachine v3 DEM, using established algorithms (e.g., D8, D-infinity) to predict subglacial channel networks, focusing on the Amundsen Sea Embayment grounding zones. These predicted pathways will guide the planning of new radar-sounding flight lines and seismic surveys using IcePod and similar systems to collect high-resolution bed elevation and substrate data. The geophysical data will be used to compute DEM error maps and to calibrate flow routing parameters (e.g., critical support area), iteratively refining both the topographic and hydrological representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, observationally constrained map of likely subglacial hydrological pathways in critical Antarctic regions, directly informing ice-sheet models. It will deliver a quantified assessment of BedMachine v3 accuracy in these dynamic zones and a calibrated workflow for efficiently targeting future geophysical campaigns to reduce topographic uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, synergistic validation strategy that uses a hydrological model to target topography validation, and then uses the validation data to improve the hydrological model itself, a coupling not previously attempted at this scale in Antarctica.

proposals.title: Inverting Bed Topography to Reconstruct Past Antarctic Ice Sheet Thickness and Grounding Line Evolution
proposals.motivation: Understanding the past configuration of the Antarctic Ice Sheet is critical for constraining its sensitivity to climate forcing and improving future sea-level projections. However, direct observations of historical ice thickness and grounding line positions are sparse. This project leverages the newly available, high-resolution Bedmap3 dataset, which provides a detailed and static record of the subglacial landscape shaped by past ice flow, to address this knowledge gap.
proposals.hypothesis: The modern bed topography of Antarctica contains a diagnostic geomorphic record that can be inverted, using ice dynamical principles, to reconstruct past ice thickness and grounding line extent during key periods of the Late Holocene and Last Glacial Maximum.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse model that couples a shallow-shelf/shallow-ice approximation ice flow model with a parameterized basal sliding law. The model will be constrained by the observed Bedmap3 bed geometry and modern surface velocities. Using control methods or adjoint-based inversion, we will solve for the past ice surface elevation and basal shear stress fields that are consistent with the formation of observed bed features (e.g., mega-scale glacial lineations, grounding zone wedges). The inversion will be applied to focus regions in West Antarctica, such as the Thwaites Glacier catchment, where contrasting melt regimes and dynamic histories are documented.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Antarctic, physics-based reconstruction of paleo-ice thickness calibrated against bed morphology. It will provide quantitative estimates of past grounding line retreat rates and ice volume change, offering a new benchmark for validating ice sheet models and clarifying the long-term context for contemporary changes observed from satellite altimetry and InSAR.
proposals.novelty: While bed shapes have been used qualitatively for paleo-reconstructions, this approach formalizes the inversion as a rigorous, quantitative dynamical problem. It moves beyond correlative mapping to a process-based recovery of ice sheet history directly from the most persistent geological record: the bed itself.

proposals.title: Causal Inversion in Ice-Ocean Coupling: Quantifying the Role of Glacier Retreat in Driving Enhanced Sub-Shelf Melt
proposals.motivation: The canonical view of marine ice sheet instability posits that ocean-driven basal melt initiates glacier retreat and thinning. However, recent observations, such as the high melt in the Thwaites grounding zone, suggest a potential feedback loop: retreat itself may alter sub-shelf cavity geometry and ocean circulation to amplify melt. Disentangling this cause-effect relationship is critical for improving prognostic models of Antarctic ice loss and sea-level rise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of observed high basal melt rates, particularly in grounding zones, is a consequence, not solely a cause, of glacier retreat and thinning, driven by dynamically induced changes in cavity geometry and access of warm water.
proposals.method: We will employ an inverse modeling framework, leveraging the new high-resolution Bedmap3 dataset for geometry. Using a time series of ice surface and grounding line positions from satellite altimetry and InSAR (1992-2025), we will first reconstruct cavity evolution for key glaciers (e.g., Thwaites, Pine Island). We will then use a 3D ocean model (MITgcm) in diagnostic mode to simulate ocean circulation and melt for each geometric snapshot. By correlating melt rate changes with geometric changes (e.g., grounding line depth, cavity volume) and applying Granger-causality statistical tests to the time series, we will quantify the fraction of melt variance explained by prior retreat.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, observationally-constrained quantification of the 'retreat-driven melt' feedback. It will directly inform coupled ice sheet-ocean models by identifying when and where this feedback is dominant, leading to more accurate parameterizations of melt in grounding zones and improved projections of future retreat.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have noted correlations, this proposal explicitly inverts the traditional causal direction and uses a formal causal inference framework on observational data to test if retreat drives melt, a non-trivial feedback not fully incorporated into contemporary ice-sheet models.

proposals.title: Inferring Oceanic Heat Transport to Antarctic Ice Shelves Using an Inverse Model Constrained by Satellite-Derived Melt Patterns
proposals.motivation: Ocean-driven basal melt is the primary cause of Antarctic ice shelf thinning and grounding line retreat, yet direct, sustained observations of the ocean properties driving this melt are sparse. This limits our ability to project future sea-level rise. An inverse method that uses the widespread, high-resolution melt patterns we can now measure from satellites to infer the underlying ocean forcing would provide a transformative constraint on ocean heat delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: The three-dimensional pattern of basal melt observed across an ice shelf contains sufficient information to inversely determine the key characteristics of the ocean circulation and thermal structure at the ice-ocean interface that produced it.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse framework coupling a plume-style melt parameterization with an adjoint model. The forward model will simulate melt rates using ocean temperature and velocity fields. The adjoint will efficiently compute the gradient of the misfit between modeled and observed melt (from IceBridge and satellite altimetry time series) with respect to ocean boundary conditions. We will apply this to Thwaites Glacier, using the high-resolution Bedmap3 topography, and solve the inverse problem using a gradient-based optimization algorithm (e.g., L-BFGS) to infer the ocean thermal forcing and flow parameters that best reproduce the observed melt regime contrasts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, spatially explicit maps of inferred ocean heat flux to the bases of critical Antarctic ice shelves like Thwaites. It will directly link observed melt heterogeneity to ocean drivers, providing a new observationally constrained benchmark for ocean and ice sheet models. The method will offer a pathway to reconstruct ocean forcing where direct measurements are absent.
proposals.novelty: While forward models predict melt from ocean conditions, inverting this process to deduce ocean properties from melt patterns is a novel, data-driven approach. It moves beyond statistical correlations to a physically based, mechanistic inference of the sub-shelf environment.

proposals.title: A Systematic, High-Resolution Map of Basal Freezing and Ice Accretion Under Antarctic Ice Shelves
proposals.motivation: While extensive research has focused on ice-shelf basal melting as a primary driver of Antarctic mass loss, the spatial distribution and magnitude of basal freezing remain poorly quantified at the continental scale. Basal accretion modifies ice-shelf structure, buttressing strength, and freshwater fluxes, yet its role in ice-sheet stability is understudied. A systematic mapping of freezing regimes is essential for improving coupled ice-ocean models and for understanding the full spectrum of ice-ocean interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that regions of significant basal freezing are widespread, structurally important, and can be systematically identified and quantified by applying advanced remote sensing techniques previously optimized for melt mapping. Specifically, we posit that freezing signatures in ice-penetrating radar and satellite altimetry data are distinct and mappable.
proposals.method: We will leverage the updated Bedmap3 topography and ice thickness datasets to provide critical geometric constraints. Using time series of satellite altimetry (CryoSat-2, ICESat-2) and ice-penetrating radar data (Operation IceBridge, ICECAP), we will apply surface-elevation-change and basal-return-power algorithms to delineate freezing zones. This approach mirrors methods used to map melt but inverts the signal analysis to identify accretion, focusing on the grounding zone and inner shelf cavities where ocean supercooling is likely.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Antarctic, observationally based map of basal freezing regimes, quantifying their contribution to ice-shelf mass balance. It will directly improve parameterizations of basal mass balance in models by providing a foundational dataset on accretion, leading to more accurate projections of ice-sheet evolution and sea-level rise.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic application of established melt-detection techniques to the inverse problem of freezing, creating a unified framework for full basal mass balance mapping. This shifts the focus from purely loss-driven analyses to a complete budget, a critical but overlooked perspective in pre-2026 ice-sheet oceanography.

proposals.title: Seasonal Modulation of Basal Melt in the Amundsen Sea Embayment: Quantifying Sub-Annual Variability from Repeat Airborne Radar Sounding
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies, including Rignot's work on Thwaites Glacier, have revealed strong spatial contrasts in melt but rely on multi-year data composites, obscuring short-term dynamics. Oceanographic models suggest seasonal pulses of warm Circumpolar Deep Water likely drive significant sub-annual melt variability at grounding zones, a key unknown for ice sheet projections. Direct, repeated observations of the ice-ocean interface on seasonal timescales are critically lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that basal melt rates in critical sectors of the Amundsen Sea Embayment (e.g., Thwaites, Pine Island) exhibit strong seasonal variability, driven by oscillations in ocean thermal forcing and cavity circulation, with melt maxima coinciding with specific phases of the austral summer oceanographic cycle.
