proposals.method: We will conduct four targeted NASA Operation IceBridge/airborne campaigns over the same flight lines in the Amundsen Sea Embayment, timed for pre-summer, peak-summer, late-summer, and winter conditions within a single year. We will employ the Multichannel Coherent Radar Depth Sounder (MCoRDS) to derive high-resolution, repeat measurements of ice thickness. Basal melt rates will be quantified by differencing sequential thickness maps and correcting for ice flow using surface velocity fields from Sentinel-1 SAR data via feature-tracking algorithms. Melt variability will be correlated with contemporaneous ocean temperature data from autonomous seals and moorings.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first direct, observationally constrained map of seasonal melt variability across key Antarctic grounding zones. It will quantify the amplitude and phasing of the sub-annual melt signal, providing a critical observational benchmark for validating and improving high-resolution, coupled ice-ocean models. The results will directly inform projections of glacier stability by clarifying the temporal sensitivity of melt to ocean forcing.
proposals.novelty: While repeat radar surveys exist, none have been designed and executed specifically to resolve *seasonal* grounding-zone melt dynamics within a single annual cycle. This moves beyond mapping mean spatial patterns to directly observing the temporal forcing function linking ocean variability to ice loss.

proposals.title: Episodic Antarctic Ice Discharge as a Driver of Non-Linear Sea-Level Rise: A Multi-Decadal Observational Analysis
proposals.motivation: Projections of future sea-level rise (SLR) often assume a constant acceleration, but recent observations of Antarctic ice shelf disintegration and glacier retreat suggest ice loss may be episodic and non-linear. This is critical because non-linear contributions from Antarctica, particularly the Amundsen Sea sector, could lead to significant underestimation of near-term SLR, impacting coastal adaptation strategies. Understanding the timing and magnitude of episodic discharge events is therefore essential for improving decadal-scale forecasts.
proposals.hypothesis: The acceleration in global mean sea-level rise is not constant but is punctuated by episodic, climatically forced pulses of ice discharge from key Antarctic basins, driven by ocean-forced grounding line retreat and ice shelf collapse.
proposals.method: We will synthesize multi-decadal (1992-2025) records of ice dynamics from satellite radar interferometry (InSAR) and altimetry to create a high-resolution time series of ice discharge for major Antarctic basins. This will integrate updated Bedmap3 topography to accurately model grounding line migration. We will apply change-point detection algorithms (e.g., PELT) and wavelet analysis to the discharge time series to identify statistically significant shifts in trend. These shifts will be correlated with ocean temperature reanalysis data and records of ice shelf break-up events to establish mechanistic drivers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first continent-wide, observationally based chronology of episodic ice discharge events and quantify their contribution to the global sea-level budget. It will directly test the constant acceleration paradigm and provide a physical framework for incorporating non-linear ice sheet processes into sea-level projections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies acceleration, this analysis uniquely seeks to decompose it into discrete, forced events linked to specific oceanic and glaciological triggers, moving beyond a simple linear trend analysis. The integration of updated basal topography with long-term discharge records provides a novel basis for detecting grounding zone instability signals.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Non-Southern Ocean Basins in Delivering Heat to Antarctic Ice Shelf Cavities
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 understanding of Antarctic ice shelf melt focuses on Southern Ocean-sourced Circumpolar Deep Water (CDW). However, global ocean heat uptake is not uniform, and deep water masses formed in the North Atlantic and Pacific may sequester substantial heat. The contribution and pathways of these non-Southern Ocean heat reservoirs to Antarctic grounding zones remain poorly quantified, representing a critical uncertainty in projecting future sea-level rise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep water masses originating from the Atlantic and Pacific basins contribute a significant fraction (≥20%) of the oceanic heat reaching the grounding lines of major Antarctic glaciers, particularly in the Amundsen and Weddell Seas, challenging the paradigm of Southern Ocean dominance.
proposals.method: We will utilize high-resolution (1/12°) global ocean reanalysis data (e.g., GLORYS12v1) and ice-ocean numerical model outputs (e.g., MITgcm) to conduct Lagrangian particle tracking experiments. Virtual water parcels will be released at the grounding lines of key glaciers (e.g., Thwaites, Pine Island) and tracked backward in time for decades using 3D velocity fields. We will apply water mass analysis (using pre-2026 climatologies like WOA18) to identify the origin basin and quantify heat content evolution along pathways. This will be integrated with updated Bedmap3 bathymetry to resolve shelf cavity circulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative partition of oceanic heat sources for Antarctic ice shelves by basin of origin. It will identify specific inter-basin pathways and timescales for heat delivery, directly informing parameterizations in large-scale ice sheet models. The results will refine projections of which glacier systems are most vulnerable to remote changes in Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation or Pacific decadal variability.
proposals.novelty: While existing studies trace CDW on the continental shelf, this proposal explicitly relaxes the assumption of a local Southern Ocean source, using Lagrangian techniques to test the non-intuitive idea that heat anomalies from distant basins are a primary driver of melt heterogeneity.

proposals.title: Partitioning Antarctic Ice Sheet Mass Loss: Quantifying the Role of Natural Oceanic Variability versus Anthropogenic Forcing
proposals.motivation: The rapid mass loss from Antarctic glaciers, particularly in the Amundsen Sea sector, is a primary contributor to global sea-level rise. While anthropogenic climate forcing is implicated, the significant decadal-scale natural variability in Southern Ocean conditions (e.g., wind patterns, sea surface temperatures, and Circumpolar Deep Water intrusions) complicates attribution. Disentangling these signals is critical for improving sea-level projections and for interpreting the observed record of glacier change, which is essential for policy and mitigation planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a substantial fraction (potentially 30-50%) of the observed dynamic ice loss from key Antarctic basins over the past three decades can be explained by low-frequency modes of natural oceanic and atmospheric variability, superimposed upon a persistent anthropogenic trend.
proposals.method: We will leverage the new high-resolution Bedmap3 topography and updated ice velocity/time series to precisely define glacier catchments and grounding lines. For each major basin, we will construct a multi-decadal record of ice shelf melt using a combination of satellite-derived surface elevation changes and ice flux divergence. This melt forcing will be statistically related to indices of natural climate variability (e.g., SAM, ENSO, IPO) and anthropogenic forcing proxies using a Bayesian hierarchical model. The model will invert the attribution problem, partitioning the variance in the melt time series into natural and forced components, while accounting for glacier response lags derived from ice flow line modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first basin-scale, observationally constrained quantification of the natural variability component in Antarctic dynamic mass loss. It will deliver a refined estimate of the committed, anthropogenic sea-level rise signal from Antarctica. The results will directly inform climate models by providing a benchmark for evaluating the simulated partitioning of natural and forced ocean melt variability.
proposals.novelty: Previous studies correlate variability with ice loss, but do not formally invert the problem to statistically partition the total observed loss. This approach integrates the latest observational constraints (Bedmap3, long-term velocity records) with a Bayesian framework specifically designed to attribute mass change, moving beyond correlation to quantitative attribution.

proposals.title: Deep Ocean Heat Intrusion and Its Role in the Accelerated Grounding Line Retreat of West Antarctic Glaciers (2000-2025)
proposals.motivation: Since 2000, satellite and in-situ observations have documented accelerated grounding line retreat and ice shelf thinning in West Antarctica, particularly in the Amundsen Sea sector. This destabilization is primarily ocean-driven, but the specific pathways, variability, and cumulative impact of deep (>700m) ocean heat uptake since 2000 remain poorly quantified, limiting our ability to project future sea-level rise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of the ocean heat responsible for Antarctic ice shelf melt since 2000 has been stored and transported in deep water masses below the pycnocline, and that this deep heat reservoir exhibits interannual variability strongly correlated with grounding line retreat rates measured by satellite radar interferometry.
proposals.method: We will synthesize all available deep hydrographic data (e.g., Argo, ship-based CTD, seal tags) from 2000-2025 to create a 4D gridded analysis of ocean temperature anomalies below 700m. This will be integrated with high-resolution ice-ocean bathymetry from Bedmap3 and MEaSUREs datasets. Using a Lagrangian particle-tracking model initialized with the deep heat anomalies, we will trace potential pathways to major grounding zones (e.g., Thwaites, Pine Island). The temporal evolution of deep heat content will be statistically correlated with time series of grounding line position derived from long-term satellite radar interferometry (InSAR) data stacks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first basin-scale, multi-decadal quantification of deep ocean heat uptake adjacent to West Antarctica and directly link its variability to observed ice sheet change. It will identify which glacier systems are most vulnerable to future pulses of deep ocean heat, thereby refining projections of sea-level rise.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies link ocean warming to melt, this proposal uniquely focuses on the *deep* (>700m) heat reservoir post-2000 and uses a direct statistical and Lagrangian modeling approach to connect its evolution to multi-decadal, satellite-observed grounding line retreat, moving beyond point-based melt correlations.

proposals.title: Beyond Buttressing Loss: Quantifying the Role of Bed Topography and Meltwater in Post-Collapse Glacier Acceleration
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm for ice shelf collapse and subsequent glacier acceleration centers on the loss of buttressing. However, observed acceleration patterns, such as those following the Wordie Ice Shelf break-up, show complex, non-uniform, and prolonged dynamic responses that are not fully explained by this single mechanism. This suggests other pre-existing or co-evolving factors, such as subglacial hydrology and bed geometry, modulate the ice flow response, a critical uncertainty for projecting future sea-level rise from Antarctica.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatial variations in bed topography and the development of subglacial meltwater systems are significant, and potentially primary, drivers of the heterogeneous and long-term dynamic instability observed following ice shelf removal, acting in tandem with the reduction in buttressing.
proposals.method: We will leverage the new high-resolution Bedmap3 datasets to perform detailed, spatially distributed ice flow model inversions for glaciers like those feeding the former Wordie Ice Shelf, using the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model (ISSM) with a higher-order physics scheme. We will conduct a suite of diagnostic and prognostic experiments: (1) isolating the mechanical effect of bed topography via sensitivity experiments with different sliding laws (e.g., Budd, Weertman), and (2) incorporating a parameterized subglacial hydrology model to simulate meltwater-driven lubrication. The model will be constrained by time series of surface velocity derived from satellite radar and optical imagery (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-1) spanning the pre- and post-collapse periods.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond the qualitative attribution of acceleration to buttressing loss by quantitatively partitioning the dynamic response into components driven by geometry, hydrology, and mechanics. It will provide a mechanistic framework to explain prolonged instability, improving the physical basis for projections of glacier evolution after future shelf collapses. The analysis will directly inform the interpretation of ongoing changes at vulnerable sites like Thwaites Glacier.
proposals.novelty: While bed topography and meltwater are known to influence flow, their specific and quantifiable role in *modulating the post-collapse acceleration trajectory*—rather than just the background state—remains unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates the new continental-scale Bedmap3 topography with a process-based analysis of a specific, decades-long observational record of instability.

proposals.title: Multi-Decadal Dynamic Response of Antarctic Outlet Glaciers Following Ice Shelf Collapse: A Remote Sensing Analysis from 2003-2025
proposals.motivation: The collapse of ice shelves like Larsen A, Larsen B, and Wordie has demonstrated that buttressing loss can trigger rapid, non-linear glacier acceleration and dynamic thinning, contributing significantly to sea-level rise. However, the long-term (>20-year) trajectory of this response—whether acceleration continues unabated, stabilizes, or decelerates—remains poorly quantified, limiting our ability to project the future of larger, at-risk systems like Thwaites and Pine Island Glaciers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that following an initial post-collapse speed-up, glacier acceleration and thinning rates will eventually stabilize or decelerate as the grounding line retreats to a new, topographically controlled position, but that the timescale and magnitude of this adjustment vary significantly between basins.
proposals.method: We will utilize a consistent, long-term remote sensing record (2003-2025) of ice velocity (NASA ITS_LIVE, ESA Sentinel-1) and surface elevation (ICESat, ICESat-2, CryoSat-2) for glaciers formerly buttressed by the Larsen A, Larsen B, and Wordie Ice Shelves. We will apply feature-tracking algorithms (e.g., auto-RIFT) to optical and SAR data to generate velocity time series, and compute elevation change rates using repeat-track analysis. These datasets will be coregistered with the new Bedmap3 topography to analyze the relationship between dynamic changes and bed geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, multi-decadal observational benchmark of glacier evolution post-collapse, directly testing numerical model projections of long-term adjustment. It will quantify the sensitivity of different glacier systems to buttressing loss and identify topographic controls on stabilization, thereby improving parameterizations for ice sheet models.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies documented the initial years of acceleration, this analysis leverages the now >20-year observational record and modern, high-resolution bed topography (Bedmap3) to systematically separate the transient collapse signal from the longer-term, topographically mediated equilibrium response across multiple sites.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Sea Level Contribution from the Accelerated Mass Loss of Thwaites Glacier, West Antarctica
proposals.motivation: Recent studies, including Rignot et al. (2019), have documented a sustained acceleration and grounding line retreat of Thwaites Glacier, driven by ocean-induced melt. This acceleration represents a major uncertainty in projections of 21st-century sea level rise. Translating the observed dynamic changes into a precise, time-evolving sea level contribution is critical for coastal planning and climate policy.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed acceleration and grounding line retreat of Thwaites Glacier since the early 1990s have already committed a significant and quantifiable sea level contribution, and the current rate of mass loss is non-linear and will increase in the near future based on the glacier's evolving geometry and melt regime.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution surface elevation changes from NASA's ICESat-2 and IceBridge with grounding line positions derived from Sentinel-1 InSAR data. Mass flux gates will be defined using the updated Bedmap3 topography. A time-dependent, flux-gate based method will be employed, calculating mass loss as the difference between ice discharge across the grounding line and modeled surface mass balance (using RACMO2.3p2). The sea level equivalent will be computed using a standard conversion (362 Gt = 1 mm SLE), with formal error propagation for all input datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a definitive, observation-based time series (1992-2025) of the sea level contribution from Thwaites Glacier, partitioning the signal into components driven by changes in ice dynamics versus surface processes. It will provide a critical observational benchmark for ice sheet models and directly inform the next IPCC assessment report.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have quantified mass loss, this analysis will uniquely leverage the latest Bedmap3 bed topography and multi-sensor data fusion to constrain the dynamic component with unprecedented accuracy, directly linking specific ocean-driven melt regimes in the grounding zone to the resulting sea level fingerprint.

proposals.title: Seismic Characterization of the Basal Interface and Subglacial Hydrology at the Thwaites Glacier Grounding Zone
proposals.motivation: The rapid acceleration and retreat of Thwaites Glacier, West Antarctica, is a primary contributor to global sea-level rise. Pre-2026 studies, including Rignot's work, have identified intense ocean-driven melt at the grounding line but lack high-resolution constraints on the glacier bed's material properties and subglacial water system. These bed conditions critically control basal friction and ice flow, yet remain poorly resolved in predictive ice sheet models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that seismic imaging will reveal a spatially heterogeneous glacier bed at Thwaites, with regions of saturated, deformable till and efficient subglacial drainage pathways that modulate basal resistance and facilitate the glacier's dynamic instability.
proposals.method: We will deploy a dense array of autonomous seismic nodes across a key transect of the Thwaites grounding zone, building on prior airborne and satellite surveys. Using active-source seismic refraction and reflection methods, we will generate high-resolution (10s of meters) 2D and 3D tomographic models of seismic P- and S-wave velocities. We will apply amplitude-versus-offset (AVO) analysis to reflection data to discriminate between hard bedrock, deforming till, and water-saturated sediments. These physical property models will be directly integrated with contemporaneous ice-penetrating radar and satellite-derived velocity maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first detailed, mechanistic map of basal material properties and subglacial hydrology for a critical sector of Thwaites Glacier. It will deliver essential, observationally constrained parameters for basal boundary conditions in ice-flow models (e.g., ISSM), directly improving projections of glacier stability and sea-level contribution. The dataset will serve as a benchmark for validating and interpreting broader-scale surveys like Bedmap3.
proposals.novelty: While seismic methods are established in glaciology, their targeted application to directly constrain the spatially variable basal controls on acceleration within the dynamically critical and difficult-to-access grounding zone of Thwaites Glacier represents a novel integration. This moves beyond mapping bed topography to diagnosing the physical *state* of the bed, a key uncertainty identified in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Damning Effect: A Hydraulic Model of Upstream Ice Flow Response to Ice Shelf Disintegration in West Antarctica
proposals.motivation: The rapid disintegration of ice shelves like Wordie and Larsen B demonstrated their critical role as buttresses, restraining upstream ice flow. However, the transient, spatially explicit hydraulic response of grounded ice to a collapsing 'dam' remains poorly quantified, limiting predictions of future sea-level rise from vulnerable basins like Thwaites and Pine Island. This study directly addresses this gap by framing the ice shelf as a hydraulic dam, linking its breach to immediate changes in subglacial water pressure and ice flux.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ice shelf removal initiates a rapid, propagating wave of increased subglacial water pressure and basal sliding upstream from the grounding line, and that the magnitude and speed of this response are governed by the pre-existing subglacial hydrological system's efficiency and the geometry of the bed.
proposals.method: We will develop a 2D coupled model integrating ice flow (using a higher-order Stokes approximation) with a dynamic subglacial hydrology module (linked cavity or porous sheet model). The model domain will be initialized using Bedmap3 topography and observed surface velocities for the Thwaites Glacier catchment. The 'dam breach' will be simulated by instantaneously removing backstress at the grounding line. We will employ the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model (ISSM) framework, adapting its control methods to solve the coupled hydraulic-mechanical problem and track the propagation of the response wave.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first mechanistic, process-based model of the immediate upstream hydraulic consequences of ice shelf loss. It will deliver quantitative estimates of the timescale and spatial pattern of accelerated ice discharge triggered by shelf disintegration, moving beyond empirical scaling laws. The results will provide a crucial physical basis for parameterizing grounding zone processes in large-scale ice sheet projections.
proposals.novelty: While the ice shelf dam analogy is established conceptually, this study is novel in its explicit, mechanistic treatment of the coupled ice-hydrology system's transient response to dam removal. It moves from a static 'buttressing' concept to a dynamic hydraulic model, a critical step for understanding the pace of ice sheet collapse.

proposals.title: Differential Dynamic Response of Tributary Glaciers to Ice Shelf Collapse: A Multi-Decadal Observational Analysis of the Larsen B and Wordie Ice Shelf Systems
proposals.motivation: The collapse of Antarctic ice shelves, such as Larsen B and Wordie, has triggered dynamic thinning and acceleration of their tributary glaciers, contributing significantly to sea-level rise. However, observations and models treat these glacier systems as monolithic entities, potentially obscuring critical heterogeneity in the response of individual tributaries. Understanding this sub-system variability is essential for improving projections of ice loss, as it may reveal stabilizing feedbacks or hidden vulnerabilities linked to bed topography and ocean forcing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tributaries within the same glacier system exhibit divergent, multi-decadal dynamic responses to ice shelf collapse, driven primarily by differences in their bed geometry (from Bedmap3) and exposure to modified ocean melt regimes following break-up.
proposals.method: We will utilize a 30-year record (1995-2025) of surface velocity and elevation from satellite radar and laser altimetry (e.g., ESA CCI, ICESat-2) to track the evolution of individual tributaries feeding former Larsen B and Wordie Ice Shelf glaciers. For each tributary, we will compute time series of dynamic thinning rates and acceleration using flux gate methods. These will be correlated with high-resolution bed topography (Bedmap3) to identify controls from bed slope and pinning points, and with ocean thermal forcing data (from ECCO2 or similar) to assess the role of melt in the newly exposed grounding zones. The analysis will focus on contrasting the temporal phasing and magnitude of response among tributaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic, observation-based assessment of intra-system glacier variability following ice shelf loss, directly testing the assumption of uniform response. It will deliver a quantified framework linking tributary-specific bed geometry to resilience, informing which branches are likely to drive future instability. The results will provide critical validation targets for higher-resolution, physics-based ice sheet models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented system-wide acceleration, the explicit scale analysis down to the tributary level, leveraging the new Bedmap3 dataset to explain differential responses, is a novel and necessary step to deconvolve the integrated signal of glacier collapse.

proposals.title: Modeling Subglacial Meltwater Pathways and Their Impact on Grounding Line Dynamics in West Antarctica
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 observations show increasing surface melt on Antarctic ice shelves and grounded ice. The subsequent routing and pressure of this meltwater at the bed is hypothesized to enhance basal lubrication and ice flow, potentially destabilizing grounding lines, but this process remains poorly quantified. This study directly addresses a key uncertainty in projecting future Antarctic Ice Sheet contribution to sea-level rise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that surface-derived meltwater, when routed to the subglacial system, creates spatially variable basal pressure anomalies that significantly alter local grounding line flux and position, with effects that propagate inland over decadal timescales.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-resolution, coupled fluid dynamics and ice flow model. Subglacial water flow will be simulated using a 2D hydraulic potential model, informed by the new Bedmap3 topography, to map drainage pathways and pressure evolution. This output will drive a basal friction parameterization in the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model (ISSM), focusing on the Amundsen Sea Embayment. The model will be constrained by InSAR-derived surface velocity maps and grounding line positions from the ITS_LIVE project.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Amundsen assessment of meltwater lubrication's role in grounding line retreat, quantifying its contribution relative to oceanic forcing. It will deliver a new, physically based parameterization for subglacial hydrology effects on ice dynamics, directly usable in continental-scale projections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links ocean melt to grounding line retreat, this proposal uniquely integrates detailed surface melt forcing with subglacial hydrology and ice dynamics to explicitly model the inland transmission of meltwater impacts, moving beyond a focus solely on the ice shelf cavity.

proposals.title: A Pan-Antarctic Ranking of Outlet Glacier Vulnerability to Ocean-Driven Instability Using a Retrograde Bed Criterion
proposals.motivation: Recent studies, including those on Thwaites Glacier, have established that ocean-induced melt at grounding zones can trigger rapid, irreversible retreat on retrograde bed slopes. However, a continent-wide assessment of which glaciers possess such unstable bed geometries is lacking, hindering projections of future sea-level rise. This gap prevents the prioritization of observational and modeling efforts on the glaciers most susceptible to marine ice sheet instability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a retrograde bed criterion to the latest, high-resolution Bedmap3 dataset will reveal a distinct, rankable distribution of vulnerability across Antarctic outlet glaciers, with a significant fraction of ice discharge originating from basins with retrograde slopes.
proposals.method: We will use the Bedmap3 ice bed, surface, and thickness datasets to calculate the bed slope (dB/dx) upstream of the current grounding line for all Antarctic outlet glaciers. A GIS-based algorithm will trace flowlines from the grounding line inland, identifying continuous retrograde bed segments. Each glacier basin will be ranked by a vulnerability index combining the length and gradient of the retrograde slope and its upstream catchment area. This analysis will be validated against observed thinning and acceleration patterns from satellite altimetry and velocity data (e.g., ITS_LIVE).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Antarctic, geometrically-based vulnerability ranking of outlet glaciers, directly quantifying the potential for ocean-driven instability. It will provide a foundational map to guide targeted field campaigns (e.g., for subglacial access or oceanographic surveys) and inform high-resolution model projections by identifying critical regions where complex grounding zone processes must be resolved.
proposals.novelty: While retrograde beds are known to be unstable, their systematic identification and ranking continent-wide using the unprecedented detail of Bedmap3 is novel. This moves beyond case studies and localized models to a quantitative, observationally grounded framework for assessing systemic risk across the entire Antarctic ice sheet.

proposals.title: Hysteresis and Re-advance Potential of Major Antarctic Outlet Glaciers Following a Hypothetical Reversal of Oceanic Forcing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Rignot's work on Thwaites Glacier, has established that ocean-driven melt at grounding lines is a primary driver of Antarctic ice sheet retreat and instability. However, projections focus almost exclusively on continued forcing. It is unknown whether a future reduction in oceanic thermal forcing could halt retreat or even initiate re-advance, a critical gap for assessing long-term ice sheet hysteresis and potential recovery pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that due to complex bed topography and ice dynamics, major Antarctic outlet glaciers like Thwaites exhibit significant hysteresis; re-advance following a reduction in oceanic forcing would be spatially heterogeneous, delayed by centuries, and potentially irreversible past certain topographic thresholds.
proposals.method: We will employ a 3D, higher-order ice flow model (e.g., ISSM) coupled to a buoyant-plume melt parameterization, initialized with Bedmap3 topography. For key basins (Thwaites, Pine Island), we will first simulate retreat under sustained oceanic forcing. We will then abruptly reverse the forcing to pre-industrial or cooler levels and simulate evolution over millennia. The model will incorporate evolving grounding line position, ice shelf buttressing, and viscoelastic bedrock response. Sensitivity experiments will test the role of bed roughness and melt parameterizations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic assessment of re-advance potential for vulnerable Antarctic glaciers, quantifying the hysteresis in the ice-ocean system. It will deliver novel metrics on recovery timescales and identify irreversible tipping points, directly informing long-term sea-level rise commitments and the interpretation of paleo-ice sheet records.
proposals.novelty: While extensive modeling exists on glacier retreat, the 'dual problem' of re-advance under reversed forcing is virtually unexplored. This study explicitly shifts the focus from vulnerability to potential recovery, a non-obvious but critical question for understanding full system dynamics.

proposals.title: Seasonal-Scale Grounding Line Dynamics in West Antarctica: Quantifying Short-Term Migration and Its Link to Oceanic Forcing
proposals.motivation: Recent work, including Rignot et al.'s studies on Thwaites Glacier, has established that ocean-driven melt at grounding zones is a primary driver of long-term Antarctic ice sheet instability. However, the short-term, intra-annual response of the grounding line to seasonal oceanographic and surface melt cycles remains poorly observed and quantified. Understanding this high-frequency migration is critical for validating and improving the temporal resolution of ice sheet models that project sea-level rise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the grounding lines of key Antarctic glaciers, such as Thwaites and Pine Island, exhibit measurable, kilometer-scale seasonal migration driven primarily by variations in oceanic heat flux and secondarily by surface meltwater-induced flexure. This short-term signal is a significant component of their long-term retreat.
proposals.method: We will apply a differential double-difference methodology to high-frequency Sentinel-1 SAR data (6-12 day repeat) from 2014-2025, using interferometry (InSAR) and amplitude tracking (offset tracking) to detect vertical tidal motion and horizontal flow changes. This will be combined with precise, high-resolution ice bed topography from Bedmap3 to accurately locate the grounding line. A novel multi-temporal stack analysis will be developed to separate the seasonal grounding line signal from long-term retreat trends and tidal aliasing, validated against concurrent ocean temperature data from instrumented seals and moorings.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Antarctic, multi-year dataset of seasonal grounding line migration, directly linking short-term ocean forcing to ice sheet dynamics. It will provide essential observational constraints for sub-annual ice sheet and ice-ocean coupling models, improving the physical basis for near-term sea-level projections.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have mapped decadal-scale grounding line retreat, this analysis focuses explicitly on the sub-annual, seasonal timescale—a critical but observationally missing link between ocean forcing and ice sheet response. The integration of high-frequency satellite data with updated Bedmap3 topography and in-situ ocean data for validation represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Atmospheric Forcing for Ice Sheet Models: A Focus on Antarctic Grounding Zone Surface Mass Balance
proposals.motivation: Accurate surface mass balance (SMB) is critical for projecting Antarctic ice sheet contribution to sea-level rise, yet regional climate models (RCMs) like RACMO and MAR operate at resolutions (~5-15 km) too coarse to resolve key drivers of SMB variability near grounding lines. These zones, where ice transitions to floating shelves, exhibit complex local wind patterns, snow redistribution, and melt processes that directly influence ice dynamics and grounding line stability, as evidenced by recent studies of Thwaites Glacier. Improved SMB estimates here are a prerequisite for reducing uncertainty in coupled ice-ocean model projections.
proposals.hypothesis: Dynamically downscaling existing RCM outputs to sub-kilometer resolution using a mesoscale atmospheric model will reveal systematic biases in accumulation and melt patterns within the Antarctic grounding zone, and that correcting these biases will significantly alter modeled ice shelf buttressing and grounding line retreat rates in key basins.
proposals.method: We will employ the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model, a pre-2026 standard, to perform nested simulations down to 500 m resolution over priority sectors (e.g., Amundsen Sea Embayment). Initial and boundary conditions will be sourced from the latest RACMO2.3p2 and ERA5 reanalysis. The core technical approach involves a two-way nesting architecture with polar-optimized physics schemes (e.g., Noah-MP land surface model, revised surface albedo parameterizations). Outputs will be validated against in-situ data from automatic weather stations, airborne radar-derived accumulation maps, and satellite-derived melt duration from scatterometers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, sub-kilometer resolution atmospheric forcing dataset specifically tailored for the Antarctic grounding zone. It will directly quantify the impact of high-resolution SMB on ice sheet model simulations, providing a crucial link between atmospheric processes and ocean-driven ice shelf melt. The results will inform the next generation of ice sheet models and reduce structural uncertainty in sea-level rise projections.
proposals.novelty: While downscaling is common, this proposal uniquely targets the dynamically critical but poorly resolved grounding zone interface, integrating the new atmospheric forcing with ice sheet models to directly assess its impact on retreat projections, moving beyond purely climatological SMB evaluation.

proposals.title: Quantifying Spatially-Varying Uncertainty in Antarctic Ice Velocity Maps Using Computer Vision Image Registration Techniques
proposals.motivation: Accurate, high-resolution ice velocity maps are critical for constraining mass balance and grounding line flux in Antarctica, yet their errors are often poorly characterized, typically represented by a single scalar. This lack of spatially-explicit error information limits the reliability of downstream analyses, such as identifying significant acceleration trends or melt patterns, particularly in dynamic regions like the Amundsen Sea Embayment. A robust, automated method to quantify pixel-level velocity uncertainty is therefore essential for advancing the interpretation of satellite-derived glacier dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that advanced image registration techniques from computer vision, specifically phase correlation and normalized cross-correlation with sub-pixel refinement, can be adapted to generate spatially-coherent error fields for ice velocity products. These error fields will reveal that uncertainty is not uniform but is systematically higher in regions of low texture (e.g., interior ice sheets) and high shear (e.g., shear margins).
proposals.method: We will adapt open-source image registration libraries (e.g., OpenCV) to process pairs of Sentinel-1 SAR and Landsat 8/9 optical imagery used in standard feature-tracking and interferometric velocity workflows. The core method involves performing multiple registrations using different algorithm parameters (e.g., correlation window sizes, search ranges) and pre-processing filters on the same image pair. The variance in the resulting displacement vectors for each pixel will be used to compute a local uncertainty estimate. We will validate the method against manually digitized features and in areas of known zero velocity (stable rock).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-source pipeline for generating spatially-explicit error maps alongside standard ice velocity products. It will directly improve the interpretation of velocity changes, especially for detecting the onset of dynamic thinning near grounding lines. The quantified uncertainties will be integrated into flux error calculations for key basins, enhancing the robustness of mass balance estimates derived from projects like ITS_LIVE and NASA MEaSUREs.
proposals.novelty: While computer vision is used to *derive* velocities, its explicit application to *quantify registration uncertainty* for glaciological products is novel. This moves beyond traditional error propagation based on manual tie-points or assumed noise models, providing a data-driven, pixel-level error directly tied to the image matching process itself.

proposals.title: Direct Coupling of Antarctic Ice-Shelf Melt to a Global Climate Model: Quantifying Feedbacks on Ocean Circulation and Sea-Level Projections
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 projections of Antarctic ice-sheet mass loss and sea-level rise remain highly uncertain, largely due to the simplified representation of ice-ocean interactions in Earth System Models (ESMs). Current ESMs often prescribe melt rates or use parameterizations not directly constrained by high-resolution observations of grounding-zone dynamics, missing critical feedbacks between meltwater discharge, ocean stratification, and large-scale circulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that directly coupling a physically-based, observationally-constrained Antarctic ice-shelf melt model to a global climate model will significantly alter projected ocean circulation patterns and associated heat delivery to the ice sheet, leading to revised estimates of centennial-scale Antarctic mass loss.
proposals.method: We will integrate the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model (ISSM), configured with Bedmap3 topography and forced by melt rates derived from satellite-derived surface-elevation-change lines and grounding-line positions, as a component into the Community Earth System Model (CESM2). The coupling will be bidirectional: CESM2 will provide ocean temperature and salinity to ISSM's ice-shelf cavity plume model, while ISSM will return spatially-distributed meltwater fluxes and iceberg discharge to CESM2's ocean component. The coupling architecture will use the Model Coupling Toolkit (MCT) to exchange fields at a daily frequency, with a focus on the Amundsen Sea sector.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first fully coupled, high-resolution assessment of Antarctic ice-ocean-climate feedbacks within a state-of-the-art ESM. It will deliver new, process-informed estimates of Antarctic contribution to sea-level rise and quantify the impact of ice-sheet melt on Southern Ocean overturning circulation and global climate sensitivity.
proposals.novelty: While offline coupling studies exist, the direct integration of a dynamic, data-assimilated ice-sheet model capable of resolving grounding-line retreat and melt heterogeneity into a global climate model represents a major advance beyond current ESM capabilities, which rely on highly parameterized ice-sheet components.

ideas.title: Pan-Arctic Ice Discharge Assessment via Distributed Sensor Network Synthesis
ideas.description: This idea expands the original 'scale up' concept by drawing an analogy from large-scale ecosystem health monitoring. The analogous problem—assessing the health and carbon flux of an entire forest continent from disparate local measurements—is solved using distributed sensor network synthesis and data fusion frameworks. Adapted to cryospheric science, the research proposes developing a Pan-Arctic Discharge Synthesis Engine (PADSE). This engine would not merely apply one method everywhere but would integrate heterogeneous data streams (satellite, model, in-situ) from all Arctic ice masses into a unified, uncertainty-aware assessment framework. The outcome is a scalable, coherent system for continuously monitoring total ice discharge across the Arctic, resolving the challenge of inconsistent methodologies and data gaps when scaling from individual glaciers to the pan-Arctic scale.

ideas.title: Inferring Basal Conditions via Velocity-Stress Phase Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption in glaciological bed inversion that a given driving stress corresponds to a unique basal slipperiness, which determines ice surface velocity. Drawing an analogy from seismology, where the relationship between fault stress drop and slip velocity is mediated by complex, rate-dependent frictional laws during an earthquake, we propose a similar paradigm shift. The analogous solution involves using the detailed temporal and spectral characteristics of seismic waves to infer dynamic friction parameters on a fault. Adapted to glaciology, the research idea is to use high-frequency time-series of surface ice velocity (from in-situ GPS or dense satellite SAR time series) not just as a scalar for inversion, but as a dynamic signal containing phase and spectral information. By analyzing velocity fluctuations in response to known forcings (e.g., tides, meltwater pulses) and fitting them to models incorporating rate-and-state or other evolving basal friction laws, we can invert for the constitutive properties of the bed itself. This moves beyond the static 'slipperiness' parameter to characterize the bed's dynamic frictional state and its transient response to stress, fundamentally challenging the assumed direct stress-velocity relationship used for bed inference.

ideas.title: Ensemble-based Bayesian inference of glaciological forcings constrained by physics and multi-source priors
ideas.description: This approach reframes the ill-posed inverse problem as a Bayesian inference task within a physics-constrained ensemble. A high-fidelity ice sheet model generates an ensemble of possible basal sliding and oceanic melt forcing scenarios, each consistent with known physical laws and auxiliary data. The system then assimilates time-series of observed surface velocity changes to update the probabilities of these ensemble members, producing a posterior distribution of the most likely forcings. This method directly yields not only a best-estimate forcing map but also robust, quantitative uncertainties, overcoming the non-uniqueness flaw of the original formulation.

ideas.title: Assess glacier vulnerability using network flow and critical path analysis
ideas.description: This research idea adapts methods from transportation engineering and network science to assess glacier vulnerability. The analogous problem—evaluating how disruptions to critical pathways affect delivery within a network—is solved by modeling flow, identifying bottlenecks, and simulating link failures. Here, Atlantic Water pathways mapped from new bathymetric data are treated as a network. Using adapted network flow algorithms, the research will model oceanic heat as 'traffic' to identify critical seabed features (canyons, sills) that control delivery to specific marine-terminating glacier basins. By simulating changes in oceanic forcing or bathymetry, the work will determine how modifications to this network alter heat transport, thereby pinpointing which glaciers are most vulnerable to shifts in Atlantic Water pathways.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Fault Zone Imaging to Grounding Line Complexity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a simple, seamless transition at the ice sheet grounding line by drawing an analogy from seismology, where the model of simple planar faults was overturned. In that field, dense sensor arrays and advanced seismic imaging revealed complex fault zone structures controlling rupture. Adapted to glaciology, the research would deploy a similar dense, multi-instrument observational network across a grounding zone. The collected data would be used to perform a high-resolution geophysical inversion, explicitly mapping the complex 3D interface—including subglacial channels, cavities, pinning points, and zones of distributed shear—that violates the simple bathymetry-topography merge and governs critical ice-ocean interaction processes.

ideas.title: Adapting Acoustic Emission Source Location for Glacial Basal Slip Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) and Non-Destructive Testing, where networks of acoustic emission sensors are used to locate and characterize micro-fracture events in engineered structures. The analogous solution involves using precise arrival times of elastic waves at multiple sensors to perform 3D source location and mechanism inversion. Adapted to glaciology, a dense array of seismometers would be deployed on a glacier to detect high-frequency seismic signals (tremors, icequakes) generated by discrete basal sliding events (stick-slip motion). By applying advanced seismic source location algorithms (like time-reversal or double-difference methods) and moment tensor analysis, originally developed for acoustic emissions and microseismicity, researchers can map the precise 3D spatial distribution, timing, and source mechanisms of basal slip events. This provides a direct, high-resolution proxy for ice flow dynamics at the bed, revealing patterns of stress accumulation and release, the heterogeneity of basal friction, and the linkage between hydrological forcing and mechanical response.

ideas.title: Applying Network Cascade Models from Epidemiology to Diagnose Glacier Acceleration Triggers
ideas.description: This research adapts solution frameworks from epidemiology and network science to investigate oceanic or atmospheric forcings causing widespread glacier acceleration. The analogous problem—determining whether a disease outbreak stems from a single source versus a widespread environmental trigger—is solved using network cascade models that analyze spatiotemporal patterns. By adapting this approach, we will model glacier termini as a network and apply statistical cascade detection methods to satellite-derived velocity time series. This will allow us to diagnose whether observed speed-ups are primarily driven by localized, chain-reaction dynamics within glacier systems or by synchronous, external forcings acting across an entire region, thereby pinpointing the dominant mechanism (oceanic or atmospheric) behind the acceleration.

ideas.title: Use subglacial flow routing to target and validate bed topography with geophysical data
ideas.description: This research direction integrates landscape evolution-based subglacial flow routing algorithms with targeted geophysical validation. It uses flow routing models on the BedMachine v3 topography to predict likely locations of subglacial channels and drainage systems. These predicted pathways then guide the planning of new, focused radar or seismic surveys in key regions to efficiently collect validation data. The resulting geophysical observations are used not only to assess the DEM's accuracy but also to evaluate and calibrate the flow routing model itself, creating a synergistic cycle that improves the physical representation of both the bed topography and the subglacial hydrological system.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Breakpoint Analysis of Ice Velocity Using Causal Inference Methods
ideas.description: This approach integrates econometric breakpoint and trend detection with glaciological principles to move beyond correlation and toward causal attribution. It first applies time-series methods to identify potential shifts in ice velocity, then uses a causal inference framework (e.g., structural causal models) to evaluate these breakpoints against a suite of physical driver datasets. The final output is a filtered set of statistically significant breakpoints that are physically explicable, directly linking velocity changes to specific environmental forcings or internal glacier dynamics. This creates a robust, interpretable bridge between statistical detection and glaciological process understanding.

ideas.title: Apply structural change detection from hydrology to ice velocity time-series
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from hydrology, where econometric-style breakpoint and trend analysis methods are already tailored for environmental time-series. Hydrologists use techniques like the Pettitt test, segmentation algorithms, and Bayesian change point models to detect structural changes in river discharge, separating climatic trends from dam operations or land-use changes. We adapt this framework to ice velocity data, applying these robust change-point detection methods to identify significant breakpoints (e.g., from surges, meltwater pulses, or calving events) and then model the distinct trend regimes between breakpoints. This provides a physically interpretable decomposition of velocity time-series into periods of stable flow, acceleration, or deceleration, directly analogous to how hydrologists partition streamflow records into natural and impacted periods.

ideas.title: Adapting Cardiac Cycle Analysis to Glacial Hydrological Pulses
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from cardiology, where the cardiac cycle is decomposed into precise temporal phases to understand physiological mechanisms. The analogous problem is studying a glacier's seasonal velocity cycle as a pulsed hydrological 'heartbeat.' By scaling down in time to sub-annual (daily/hourly) resolution and deploying a synchronized multi-sensor array (GPS, seismometers, pressure sensors, discharge gauges), we can capture the high-resolution waveforms of velocity, water pressure, and meltwater input. The adapted solution is to analyze these synchronized data streams to decompose the annual velocity cycle into distinct mechanistic phases, establishing precise temporal causality between meltwater pulses, subglacial plumbing system pressurization, and ice flow response, much like correlating electrical signals, pressure changes, and blood flow in a heartbeat.

These insights then guide the creation of high-fidelity synthetic test beds that embody these challenging geophysical scenarios. The BedMachine methodology is then applied to these synthetic terrains under controlled conditions, rigorously evaluating and improving its interpolation and inversion algorithms for robustness across diverse real-world conditions.

ideas.title: Extend velocity time series using paleoclimate proxy reconstruction methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts the paleoclimate method of extending time series using proxy data to the problem of extending a geophysical velocity time series beyond its original 2005 endpoint. In paleoclimatology, instrumental records are extended by calibrating proxies like tree rings to measured temperature during an overlap period and then applying that relationship to older proxy data. Here, we identify a modern, continuously measured proxy variable (e.g., from continuous GPS networks or satellite data) that is physically correlated with the target velocity signal. We calibrate a statistical model (a transfer function) linking this proxy to the original velocity series for the period where both datasets exist (e.g., 1990-2005). We then apply this calibrated model to the proxy data collected after 2005 to generate a statistically reconstructed extension of the velocity time series. This reconstructed series allows for an assessment of trends beyond the original data cutoff, providing a forward-looking analysis despite the lack of direct long-term measurements.

ideas.title: Bayesian Data Assimilation for Uncertainty-Aware Reconstruction of Evolving Bed Topography
ideas.description: This method develops a stochastic, state-space framework for reconstructing time-evolving bed topography by integrating physics and sparse data. It formulates time-dependent sediment mass conservation (the Exner equation) as a stochastic process model that predicts bed change, while explicitly accounting for model and parameter uncertainty. Sparse, noisy measurements of bed or water surface elevation are assimilated sequentially using a Bayesian filter (e.g., Ensemble Kalman Filter) to optimally correct the predicted bed state and provide uncertainty estimates. This approach ensures robust, physically plausible reconstructions even with gappy data, as it prevents error accumulation and quantifies confidence in the evolving topographic map.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Observational Framework to Mechanistically Link Sub-Satellite Processes to Large-Scale Acceleration
ideas.description: This research integrates satellite remote sensing with strategically co-located in-situ observations to move beyond correlation and establish mechanistic links. The design employs targeted field campaigns using autonomous platforms to directly measure the sub-satellite-scale processes (e.g., eddy kinetic energy fluxes, submesoscale fronts) hypothesized to drive large-scale changes. By analyzing these concurrent, multi-scale datasets, the study can quantify the energy and momentum transfers between scales. This approach directly tests causality and isolates the specific dynamical pathways through which small-scale processes modulate the observed large-scale acceleration signal.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Waveform Inversion for Time-Dependent Bedform Evolution
ideas.description: This idea challenges the steady-state mass conservation assumption in bed topography mapping by drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic imaging. In Full-Waveform Inversion (FWI), the time-dependent propagation of seismic waves is used to iteratively reconstruct a detailed, evolving model of subsurface properties, moving beyond static snapshots. We adapt this solution by developing a time-dependent mass conservation inversion method. The core is a numerical model that solves the Exner equation (sediment continuity) forward in time. Sequential topographic datasets (e.g., from multibeam sonar or lidar) serve as the time-lapse observational constraints, analogous to seismic traces. An adjoint-based optimization scheme iteratively adjusts the spatio-temporal fields of sediment flux and initial bed conditions to minimize the misfit between simulated and observed bed evolution, thereby dynamically mapping the changing topography and its governing transport processes.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Joint Inversion Framework for Multi-Physics Geophysical Data Integration
ideas.description: This research develops a robust data integration system that overcomes the limitations of direct image fusion by employing a joint inversion methodology. Instead of merging disparate data sets directly, the system inverts all geophysical observations simultaneously to estimate a consistent 3D model of subsurface physical properties (e.g., density, velocity, resistivity). The inversion is constrained by structural coupling terms and geological prior information to ensure a geologically sensible result. The final output is a unified earth model that honors the full resolution and physical meaning of each input data type, providing a more reliable basis for interpretation than a simple fused image.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Horizon Tracking for Automated Bedform Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from seismic interpretation in geophysics, where CNNs are used to automatically track continuous geological horizons. The analogous solution involves training networks on manually interpreted seismic layers to learn the amplitude and texture patterns of continuous surfaces. Adapted to geomorphology, the research would employ a similar CNN architecture, but train it on manually mapped bedform crests and troughs from bathymetric/topographic data. The network would learn to identify and trace the continuous spatial lines of features like sills and troughs based on their characteristic elevation gradients and curvatures, outputting vectorized or rasterized line maps of these features rather than simple pixel-wise classifications, thereby providing a more geomorphologically coherent and directly usable output for analysis.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Self-Supervised Learning for Robust Bedform Feature Extraction
ideas.description: This redesigned system employs a hybrid architecture that combines a convolutional neural network with a physics-based model of bedform evolution. It is trained in a self-supervised manner using synthetically generated bedform fields constrained by hydrodynamic principles, alongside unlabeled real-world topographic data. The network learns to extract features by predicting flow fields or sediment flux from topography, enforcing physical consistency. This approach creates a more robust and generalizable model that can identify sills, troughs, and other bedforms across diverse environments without requiring extensive manual labels.

ideas.title: Apply astronomical multi-wavelength data fusion to geophysical datasets
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from astronomy/astrophysics, where multi-wavelength data fusion is routine. Astronomers combine observations from telescopes across the electromagnetic spectrum using advanced registration and pixel-level fusion algorithms to align datasets with different resolutions and noise characteristics, creating composite views that reveal structures invisible in any single band. The adapted research applies these astronomical fusion pipelines to disparate geophysical datasets (seismic, gravity, magnetic, electromagnetic). It involves developing a geophysical coordinate framework and implementing wavelet-based fusion algorithms optimized for each modality's noise and resolution, creating unified volumetric models where complementary data constraints are simultaneously visualized and analyzed for subsurface characterization.

ideas.title: Adapting Vascular Network Growth Algorithms for Subglacial Drainage Routing
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting computational algorithms from the field of angiogenesis (blood vessel growth) modeling to improve hydrological flow routing for subglacial water drainage. In computational biology, vascular network growth algorithms successfully simulate how transport networks form and evolve in response to environmental gradients within a changing tissue matrix. The analogous solution involves rules for pathfinding, branching, and network connection based on stimuli like chemical gradients. This solution can be adapted by using hydraulic potential derived from a landscape evolution model's new topography as the primary gradient. The adapted research idea would develop a flow routing algorithm where drainage pathways are not statically calculated but dynamically 'grow' and adapt. These pathways would initiate from melt sources, propagate along the steepest hydraulic gradient, branch under simulated water pressure regimes, and connect to form efficient channel networks, thereby simulating the evolution of subglacial drainage systems in a more biologically-inspired, dynamic way.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Geophysical Feature Learning via Deep Fusion Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a unified deep learning framework that integrates medical-grade multi-modal image fusion with convolutional neural network (CNN) feature extraction for geophysical analysis. The system first employs advanced non-rigid registration and pixel-level fusion techniques to create a coherent 3D data volume from disparate sources like seismic, bathymetric, and magnetic surveys. A specialized CNN is then trained on this fused volume to automatically detect and classify subtle bedforms and geological structures. Crucially, the framework is iterative: the CNN's learned feature representations are fed back as constraints to refine the initial data alignment, creating a synergistic loop that continuously improves both data fusion quality and feature detection accuracy beyond what either step could achieve independently.

ideas.title: Structural Break Detection and Regime-Specific Trend Analysis for Extended Velocity Series
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple temporal extension by first applying multiple structural breakpoint detection algorithms (e.g., Bai-Perron tests, CUSUM) to the full, updated velocity time series to identify significant changes in mean, variance, or trend. For each statistically homogeneous segment identified, including the post-2005 period, regime-specific trend models are fitted and compared. The core analysis then tests whether the trend mechanism post-2005 is consistent with the pre-2005 regime or represents a significant departure, ensuring conclusions about recent trends are robust to underlying non-stationarities.
